steps.m: Two special letters (Ta Ha) that begin this surah
steps.n: This is one of the special letter combinations that appear at the beginning of some surahs in the Quran. While we don't know their exact meaning, we read them as individual letters: ط is pronounced 'Ta' and ه is pronounced 'Ha'
This is a negative particle that makes the meaning 'not'. It's one of the basic words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.

The alif is written as alif mamduda (آ) instead of regular alif (ا). This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it negates the verb أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down), making the meaning 'We did not send down'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he caused to send down (acted on القرءان)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the start makes it causative - someone is making something come down. Here, القرءان (the Quran) is what's being sent down.
Adding نَا shows we (multiple people including the speaker) did the action
This is one of the basic prepositions in Arabic that shows something is on top of or upon something else. It doesn't come from a root.
The ى changes to يْ when we add something to the end. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: upon you (object of أَنزَلْنَا - we sent down)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is happening to 'you'. In this ayah, this connects to the verb أَنزَلْنَا (we sent down) to show what was sent down upon.

steps.m: to read or recite something out loud

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes to pattern فُعْلان to show this is not just any reading but a complete/abundant recitation

steps.m: the Quran (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: Added 'the' at the beginning to make it specific, and fatha at the end to show it's the object of 'We sent down' in the verse
Root letters got vowels (fat-ha marks) to make it mean 'he struggled' in the past
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and changed vowels for present tense

steps.m: so that you struggle/face hardship (purpose of ما أَنزَلْنَا)
steps.n: Added لِ to show this explains the purpose of the previous statement 'We did not send down'. This word explains why the Quran was NOT sent down.
Added ى (alif maqsurah) to complete the verb. This is how verbs ending in weak letters are written.
This is the base form of the exception particle. It shows that what comes after it is different or excluded from what came before.
Adding ا at the end makes the word flow better when connecting to what comes after it. In this ayah, it's excluding تَذْكِرَةً (a reminder) from something mentioned before.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'reminder' by adding تَ at start, changing vowels, and adding ة at end
Added tanween (ً) to show this is the object of إِلَّا (except), meaning 'except as a reminder'
Added the preposition لِ (for/to) to مَن (whoever) to show purpose - for whom something is meant
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning. This word connects to تَذْكِرَةً (reminder), showing who the reminder is for.

steps.m: to fear with respect and awe

steps.n: Adding vowels to make past tense verb showing feeling
Added يَ at start to show it's happening now. The ي at the end changed to ى because it's a special type of verb (called weak)
Added the small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long vowel sound
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The pattern تَفْعِيل shows that something is being done repeatedly or gradually

steps.m: a gradual sending down (as the object being acted upon)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added the question word مَنْ (who) after مِنْ (from)

The ن of مِنْ combines with the م of مَنْ to create a doubled م (shown by the shadda). This is a pronunciation change that doesn't affect meaning. This word refers to the one who created (خَلَقَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to create from nothing, to make something new

steps.n: Added fathas (َ) to make it 'he created' in the past tense. This shows Allah is the one who did the creating.
Added marks (fatha and sukun) to make it readable - this is the basic form of the word for 'earth'

steps.m: the earth (object of خَلَقَ - He created)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific ('the earth') and fatḥa at the end shows it's receiving the action of creation
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Makes the word plural by adding ات. The و appears because this is from a root ending in و
Makes the word definite by adding ال and doubles the س with shadda
Adds و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Adds kasra at the end to show connection to the word العُلَى (the highest) that follows it. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he rose' to 'highest' by making it feminine superlative (like saying 'most high')

steps.m: the highest (describing السَّمٰوٰتِ 'the heavens' mentioned before)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it definite. This word describes 'the heavens' mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changes from 'he was merciful' to 'very merciful one' by adding -aan ending to show lots of mercy
Adding the definite article makes it refer to Allah specifically

steps.m: The Most Merciful (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Changed alif to wasl-alif, added elongated alif (ٰ) and final damma to show this is a divine name
Basic form of the preposition that shows position or location. In this ayah, it connects الرحمن (The Most Merciful) to العرش (the throne).
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha (َ) on ع and ل, and standing alif (ى) at the end which makes a long 'aa' sound.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and form the noun 'throne'. The word becomes a concrete noun representing an elevated seat of authority.
Added the definite article 'the' and kasra at the end because it comes after the word عَلَى (upon) in the ayah

steps.m: to be level, balanced, or complete

steps.n: Root letters س-و-ي were joined with vowels (َ) to make the word 'he made level'

steps.m: he became established/settled (subject of عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ)
steps.n: Added است at the beginning to show the action is done thoroughly or completely by the subject. This word connects to عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ (upon the throne) in the ayah.
Changed the spelling of the initial alif to show it connects smoothly with previous words when reading
Added alif maksura with superscript alif to show the final long vowel sound
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: to/for him (possesses مَا فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ - what is in the heavens)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (him) to show who something belongs to or is for. This word shows that everything mentioned after (what is in the heavens, earth, etc.) belongs to Him (Allah).

steps.m: to/for him (possesses مَا فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ - what is in the heavens)
steps.n: Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to all things in the heavens and earth, as shown by the context 'whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth'
This is a basic preposition showing location or position
Added kasra to show it's connecting to the word after it. This preposition is used here to show location - specifically what exists in the heavens.
Changed the final ي to ى which is a common spelling variation at the end of words. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to rise up, be elevated, or be high

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high above us
Makes it plural by adding the feminine plural ending ات
Makes the word definite by adding ال

steps.m: of the heavens (possessed by/in/belonging to something)
steps.n: Adding the -i ending shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
This is a question word that asks about things (not people). In this ayah, it's being used to mean 'whatever' or 'all that'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects 'what' (ما) with 'the heavens' (ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ) to mean 'what is in the heavens'.
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added kasra at the end to show this word comes after the word فِي (in/on) in the ayah
This is a question word that asks about things (not people). In this ayah, it's being used to mean 'whatever' or 'what' is between the heavens and earth.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, linking 'what is in the heavens' with 'what is between them'
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a preposition meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ and الْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا at the end to show this means 'between them both', referring to the heavens and earth mentioned in the ayah
This is a question word that asks about things (not people). In this ayah, it's being used to mean 'whatever' or 'all that' is in a place.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous one, showing that we're listing multiple things that belong to Allah.
This is a particle that shows location, specifically indicating something is beneath or under something else
Adding vowel marks to make the word readable and give it its proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun meaning 'moist soil'
Added shadda to emphasize the moistness quality of the soil
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added alif maksura at the end to show the proper spelling of this word when it appears at the end of a phrase
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. Here it connects to the verb تَجْهَرْ (you speak openly) to say 'if you speak openly'.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it
Added basic past tense form by connecting letters with fatha

steps.m: you speak loudly/openly (with بِٱلْقَوْلِ - in speech)
steps.n: Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action. Changed vowels to show it's a present tense action. This verb connects with بِٱلْقَوْلِ which specifies that the speaking is 'in speech'.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'speech'. This word is what you're speaking openly about.
Added ال to show we're talking about specific speech
Added بِ at the start to show how the speaking is done. The alif in ال changes to ٱ after بِ because of how Arabic connects words.

steps.m: with/by the speech (object of تَجْهَرْ - speak openly)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end because it's the object (thing being acted upon) after the word بِ (with/by). This shows what is being spoken openly.
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement

steps.m: then indeed he (subject of يَعْلَمُ - knows)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who the statement is about. This pronoun refers to Allah and is the subject of يَعْلَمُ (knows) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he knows (the subject of this verb is Allah, who knows السِّرَّ - the secret)
steps.n: Changed from past tense 'knew' to present tense 'knows' by adding يَ at the start. The ُ at the end shows Allah is doing the action of knowing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the first vowel from fatha to kasra to make it into a noun meaning a state of being hidden
The word gets 'the' (ال) in front and some letters combine together. It's the thing that Allah knows in this ayah

steps.m: the secret (object of يَعْلَمُ - what Allah knows)
steps.n: The word gets a fatHa mark because it's the object (what Allah knows)
Connected the root letters with vowels to form basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he hid' to mean 'more/most hidden'. Like saying something is 'more secret' than a secret. The أ at the start and ى at the end make it comparative.
Adds connecting 'wa' to link with previous word السِّرَّ (the secret)
Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel marks
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
This is a negative particle that makes statements negative, like 'not' in English
Added fatha to show this is being used to completely negate something that comes after it. In this ayah, it's negating إِلٰهَ (deity/god) to say 'there is no god'.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show emphasis in the negation. This is a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed the first letter to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to make the 'aa' sound longer
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of لَآ (no/there is no)

This is a special word that shows something is different or separate from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it's used to say that after saying 'there is no god', Allah makes an exception by saying 'except Him'.
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'he'
Added the vowel marks هُ (hu) and وَ (wa) to show proper pronunciation. In this ayah, هُوَ refers back to اللَّهُ (Allah) mentioned just before it.
Added fatha to show this is connected to what follows
Added هُ (him) to show who something is for. This word connects to اللَّهُ (Allah) in this ayah, showing what belongs to Him.

steps.m: to name, to mark, to designate

steps.n: Root letters س-م-و became plural 'names' by adding أ at start and changing middle vowels
Added the definite article ٱلْ to make it specific and lengthened the alif
Added the ُ (damma) to show this is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic adjective form
Changed the vowels and added ى at the end to make it feminine and mean 'most beautiful/best' (like English -est)
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and added ٰ to show it's a special form used for Allah's names
This is a question word that starts yes/no questions, like 'did' or 'does' in English

steps.m: and did? (asking أَتَىٰكَ - did it come to you?)
steps.n: The و at the start connects this question to what came before it
Added alif maksura marker (ٰ) to show the proper pronunciation of the final ى

steps.m: has come to you (object of هَلْ)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show who is being addressed (you). Together with هَلْ before it, this forms the question 'Has (it) come to you?'
Changes from a verb (to tell) into a noun (the thing that is told)
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb أَتَىٰكَ (came to you) in this ayah
This is a proper name that was borrowed from Hebrew into Arabic. It does not follow standard Arabic morphological patterns.
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation of the name
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) to show the extended alif sound at the end
Added small alif (ٓ) to indicate a slight pause in recitation
This is a time word that shows when something happened in the past. In this ayah, it introduces when Musa saw the fire.

steps.m: to see with one's eyes or mind

steps.n: Added fathas to show this happened in the past and 'he' did it
The alif at the end is written differently because it's at the end of the word
The weak letter و changes to ا in this pattern
The ًا at the end shows this is indefinite (any fire) and is being acted upon - in this case, being seen by Musa
Added vowels to show this is a past-tense verb. The middle letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle.

steps.m: then he said (connecting to رَءَا/he saw before it)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after he saw the fire. This connects the two actions: first he saw (رَءَا), then he said (فَقَالَ).
Basic noun pattern with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter, turning the concept of dwelling into the people who dwell together.
Added لِ meaning 'to' at the beginning. The ِ at the end shows this word follows the لِ.

steps.m: to his family (who he spoke to in قَالَ)
steps.n: Added هِ meaning 'his' at the end. This shows Musa was speaking to his own family.

steps.m: to stay or remain in a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he stayed' to 'stay!' by adding U sound at start and changing middle vowel - makes it a command

steps.m: all of you stay! (command to group)
steps.n: Added plural ending to make the command address multiple people
This is a particle of emphasis
Added shadda for emphasis

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to أَهْلِهِ - his family)
steps.n: Attached pronoun suffix changes meaning to 'indeed I'

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to أَهْلِهِ - his family)
steps.n: Added vowel marks and elongation mark (ىٓ) for proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after hamza to strengthen the meaning, showing clear or definite perception
Added تُ at the end to show that 'I' did the action. This verb is acting on the word نَارًا (fire) in the ayah.
Root letters ن-و-ر became the noun 'fire' by changing و to ا and adding fatha (short 'a' sound) on ن
Added tanween (ًا) to show it's 'a fire' - makes it indefinite/non-specific
This is a particle expressing hope or expectation
Added shadda for emphasis, making the meaning stronger

steps.m: surely perhaps I (will bring you from it)
steps.n: Added ي with kasra before it to show this is about 'me/I'

steps.m: surely perhaps I (will bring you from it)
steps.n: Added initial shadda and elongation mark (ىٓ) for emphasis in recitation
Added hamza with fatha and long alif at start to show first person present tense. Added kasra under ت to complete the present tense form.

steps.m: I will bring to you all (addressing Musa's family)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show who is being addressed. In this ayah, Musa (AS) is speaking to his family about bringing something from the fire he saw.
This is a preposition

steps.m: from it (referring to نَارًا - the fire)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the fire (نَارًا) that Musa saw

steps.m: from it (referring to نَارًا - the fire)
steps.n: Added shadda on م for emphasis and smooth pronunciation when connected to previous word

steps.m: to take/bring a flame or knowledge from a source
a flame or piece of knowledge taken from a source
steps.n: Changed from a root meaning 'to take fire/knowledge' into a noun meaning 'the actual fire/knowledge piece taken' by adding the vowel marks

steps.m: with a flame (from the fire mentioned)
steps.n: Added بِ to show this is the thing Musa will bring back, connecting it to the fire mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: with a flame (referring to an unspecified amount from the fire)
steps.n: The ٍ shows this is any portion of the fire, not a specific amount

steps.m: or (connecting two possibilities: bringing an ember OR finding guidance)
steps.n: This is a basic conjunction particle that gives a choice between alternatives. In this ayah, it connects two possibilities that Musa (AS) hoped for: either bringing back an ember from the fire OR finding guidance at the fire. It has no root since it's a particle.

steps.m: or (connecting two possibilities: bringing an ember OR finding guidance)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha (َ) on أ and sukun (ْ) on و
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels - the middle vowel 'i' shows it's about finding something
Changed from past to present tense by replacing وَ with يَ at start and changing ِدَ to دُ at end

steps.m: I find (referring to finding guidance at the fire)
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'I' by replacing يَ with أَ at the beginning

steps.m: upon (connects النَّارِ 'the fire' to أَجِدُ 'I find')
steps.n: Basic preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else. Here it connects 'I find' with 'the fire' to say 'I find upon the fire'. The ى at the end shows this word connects to what comes after it.
The root 'n-w-r' (light) becomes 'naar' (fire) - they are actually different words, not just an emphasis change
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
The kasra indicates the word is in the genitive case. Here it comes after the preposition على (on/upon), which always puts the following word in the genitive case.

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to guide' into the noun 'guidance'
The word gets a special mark (tanween) at the end to show it means divine guidance from Allah, not just regular guidance

steps.m: when (connecting to أَتَىٰهَا - he came to it)
steps.n: This is a time particle that connects two events - here it connects to the next word أَتَىٰهَا (he came to it)

steps.m: and/then when (connecting to أَتَىٰهَا - he came to it)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is part of a sequence - one thing happening after another. Here it connects what happened when Moses came to the fire.
The elongated alif (آ) at the end is a common Quranic orthographic variation for emphasis in pronunciation, without changing the meaning
This is a basic verb with a weak letter ي at the end

steps.m: he came (before نُودِىَ - he was called)
steps.n: The root letters got vowel marks and the last letter changed to a special ى (looks like a stick lying down) to show it happened in the past. This word comes before 'he was called' in the verse.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced like a long a sound

steps.m: he came to it/her (referring to the fire mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show he came to something feminine (referring back to the fire mentioned earlier in the story)
This is a basic verb with a weak letter ي at the end
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he was called (before يٰمُوسَىٰٓ - O Moses)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice to show Moses was being called, not doing the calling. This connects to the next word يٰمُوسَىٰٓ (O Moses) who is being called.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
يٰ is added at the start to call out to Moses directly, like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!'. The ٰٓ at the end shows a slight pause in the calling.
This is the basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger and more certain

steps.m: indeed I am (subject of رَبُّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show this is about me (first person). The shadda (ّ) gets stronger because it's connecting to the ي. This word connects to رَبُّكَ (your Lord) to say 'Indeed I am your Lord'
Changed the ي to ى with ٓ (alif khanjariyah) on top. This is just a writing style change that doesn't affect the meaning
This is one of the basic pronouns in Arabic that means 'I'. The vowel marks help with pronunciation.
Added the alif at the end to complete the pronoun. In this ayah, Allah is speaking to Prophet Musa, saying 'Indeed, I am your Lord.'

steps.m: to take care of, raise, nurture

steps.n: The doubled ب (with shadda) shows this is someone who takes complete and perfect care of others

steps.m: Lord (subject of إِنِّي أَنَا - Indeed, I am)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is completing 'Indeed, I am...' to say 'Indeed, I am Lord'

steps.m: your Lord (subject of إِنِّي أَنَا - Indeed, I am)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end shows this is YOUR Lord. This completes the statement 'Indeed, I am your Lord' which Allah is saying to Musa (Moses)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he took off' to 'take off!' by adding alif at start and making it a command

steps.m: so take off! (command to Moses to remove his shoes)
steps.n: Adds فَ at beginning to show this command follows from the previous statement where Allah identifies Himself as Moses's Lord
Changed the root into a noun meaning one sandal. This is what Moses was commanded to remove.
Dual form to indicate two sandals
Adding possessive pronoun 'your' to the dual form. This word is the direct object (what is being removed) of the command 'take off' (فَٱخْلَعْ).
This is the emphatic particle إِنَّ that adds emphasis to what follows, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'
Adds كَ at the end to show this is addressing 'you'. In this ayah, Allah is speaking directly to Prophet Musa.

steps.m: to flow or move (like water in a path)
valley (literally: a place where water flows)
steps.n: Added ا after و to create a noun meaning 'place where water flows', which came to mean 'valley'

steps.m: the valley (the specific valley being addressed)
steps.n: Added ال to show this is referring to a specific valley being discussed

steps.m: in the valley (location where Allah is speaking to Moses)
steps.n: Added بِ at the start to show location - this is the sacred valley where Moses received revelation
Adding مُ at the start and doubling the middle letter (د) makes it mean 'something that was made holy'
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning to indicate something thoroughly or completely sanctified
Adding the definite article makes this refer to the specific holy valley mentioned in the ayah
Adding kasra shows this word describes and belongs to ٱلْوَادِ (the valley), forming the phrase 'the holy valley'

steps.m: to fold or roll up (like folding cloth or rolling up a scroll)

steps.n: Changed from root letters to proper name Tuwa (a sacred valley) by adding vowel marks

steps.m: Tuwa (name of sacred valley mentioned in the Quran)
steps.n: Added tanween (ً) and alif maqsura (ى) at the end to complete the proper noun form. This is the name of the blessed valley where Allah spoke to Prophet Musa.
This is a basic pronoun that means 'I'
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb ٱخْتَرْتُكَ (I chose you) in this ayah.

steps.m: to choose or select what is best
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added reflexive pattern to show the action is done for oneself
Changed middle weak letter to match Form VIII pattern
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding -tu ending
Added second person object pronoun and changed initial alif. This word follows وَأَنَا (and I) in the ayah.
This is the basic meaning showing the action of hearing or listening
Adding the vowel marks changes it from a root to a complete past tense verb meaning 'he heard'
Added است at the beginning to show trying hard to do the action
Changed from 'he listened' to 'you listen!' by changing the middle vowel from 'a' to 'i' to make it a command
Added فَ at the beginning to connect it to what came before, meaning 'so' or 'then'
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' - in this ayah it refers to what is being revealed
The لِ at the start shows purpose - here it connects 'listen' (فَٱسْتَمِعْ) to 'what is revealed'

steps.m: to reveal or inspire divine guidance
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from active 'he revealed' to passive 'being revealed' by adding يُ at start. The ى ending shows ongoing action.
The small alif (ٰ) is added above the ى to help with pronunciation
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger. Here it emphasizes 'I am Allah' in the ayah.
Added نِ to show this is about 'me/I'. This connects to the next part of the ayah where Allah says 'I am Allah'.
Added ىٓ at the end to make the word sound complete when you stop on it. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: I (the speaker in لَآ إِلٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠)
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun meaning 'I'. In this ayah, Allah is referring to Himself, emphasizing there is no deity except Him.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added the u-vowel at the end to show this is the subject
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific
This is the basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
The alif is elongated with a madda mark to emphasize the negation. This word negates إِلٰهَ (deity) in the phrase 'there is no deity except Me'

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Changed أ to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'something that is worshipped'

steps.m: any deity (object of لا/there is no)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being affected by لا (there is no). The small alif (ٰ) represents a long 'aa' sound.
This is the basic form of the exception particle
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning
The long alif at the end adds extra emphasis and helps with pronunciation in Quranic recitation
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'I'
Added a small mark (۠) above the final alif to indicate emphasis in pronunciation. This is used here because it's part of Allah's speech emphasizing 'Indeed, I am Allah'
The root conveys the concept of worship or service
Changed to command form by adding specific vowel pattern
Added helper alif to make pronunciation easier
Added فَ to show this command follows from what was said before
Added نِ to show who should be worshipped (me)
Added ي to complete the meaning of 'me' as the one being worshipped
The root means to stand or establish
Basic command form where the weak letter و is dropped. This is a common pattern with hollow verbs (verbs with و in the middle).
Added hamza with fatha at beginning to help pronunciation of command form
Added kasra at end to show this is a command
Added و with fatha to mean 'and'. This word is commanding to establish الصَّلَوٰةَ (the prayer).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (praying) into a noun meaning one prayer, by adding ة at the end
Makes the word definite by adding ال, showing we're talking about a specific prayer
Shows this is what's being acted upon by the command 'establish' (أَقِمِ) in the ayah
The root relates to remembrance or mention
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'the act or instance of remembering/mentioning'
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for/to' and kasra at end because it's connected to the next word
Added ى to show Allah is speaking about His own remembrance
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's used at the start of sentences to make what follows more emphatic.
Added kasra (ِ) under the إ to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added shadda (ّ) on ن to make the emphasis stronger. This word emphasizes 'the Hour' (ٱلسَّاعَةَ) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to move or measure through time

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding vowels and turning و into ا
Adds ة to make it a noun referring to one specific instance of time
Makes the word definite by adding ال, also causes first letter to get shadda

steps.m: the Hour (as the object of إِنَّ)
steps.n: Adds fatha at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily)
Added long alif and kasra to create active participle (someone/something that does the action)
Added feminine marker ة to make the word refer to something feminine
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' instead of 'the')
Connected the root letters with vowels. The weak letter و changes to ا in this form.

steps.m: I am about to (connected to أُخْفِيهَا - I hide it)
steps.n: Changed to present tense first person singular. This verb connects to the next word أُخْفِيهَا (I hide it), showing what the speaker is about to do.

steps.m: to hide or conceal something from view

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added أُ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action, and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: I hide it (referring to الساعة/the Hour)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show what is being hidden. Here it refers back to الساعة (the Hour) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to give what is due (reward or punishment)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: she/it is given what is due
steps.n: Changed from 'he gave' to 'is given' by adding تُ at start and changing vowels - makes it passive and happening now

steps.m: so that she/it is given what is due
steps.n: Added لِ to show purpose - this happens in order for something else to happen

steps.m: so that she/it is given what is due (referring to كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ - every soul)
steps.n: Added alif maksura at the end to show this is connected to 'every soul' mentioned right after in the ayah
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in تَسْعَىٰ (strives/seeks)
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to make a noun. This word is what receives the action in تُجْزَىٰ (is rewarded/recompensed).
Added the ٍ mark (kasratin) to show this word is receiving the action - it's the soul that is being rewarded
This is a relative pronoun that means 'what' - it connects to the verb تَسْعَىٰ (strives/seeks) to mean 'what (it) strives for'
Added بِ at the beginning to show how or by what means something happens. Here it connects to تَسْعَىٰ (strives) to show 'by what it strives for'
Connected the root letters and added fathas. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Adding ت changes it from 'he' to 'she'
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it's used with يَصُدَّنَّكَ (prevent you) to say 'do not let prevent you'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or sequence from what came before. It connects this statement to the previous context.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Doubled the middle letter to show intensity and merged the last two letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing first vowel to damma
Added نَّ for emphasis - this makes the action more certain or definite

steps.m: he definitely prevents/blocks you (object of فَلَا)
steps.n: Added كَ to show who is being prevented. This word is the object of فَلَا (so do not) in this ayah.
This is a preposition that shows moving away from something or being distant from it. In this ayah, it connects with يَصُدَّنَّكَ (prevent/turn away) to show what someone is being turned away from.
Added هَا which refers back to belief/faith mentioned in the context. The whole phrase يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْهَا means 'turn you away from it (belief)'.
This is a relative pronoun that refers to any person. In this ayah, it refers to the person who doesn't believe, connecting to the verb يُؤْمِنُ (believes).
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not'
The ل gets a shadda (double letter mark) to add emphasis. This emphasizes the negation of belief (يُؤْمِنُ) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make past tense verb
Added an alif at start to make the meaning stronger - like going from 'believe' to 'truly believe/have faith'
Changed from past tense (he believed) to present tense (he believes) by adding يُ at the start. The person doing this action is the one who tries to prevent you, mentioned in يَصُدَّنَّكَ
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or where it happens
Added هَا which refers back to something feminine mentioned before (in this case, it refers to the same thing as عَنْهَا in the ayah)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added اِ at start and doubled the ت to show doing something with extra effort or purpose
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it in the verse. The word هَوَىٰهُ (his desires) comes right after this word showing what he followed.
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Added هُ at the end to mean 'his'. This word is being acted upon by the verb اتَّبَعَ (followed) in the phrase 'and followed his desire'.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from 'he' to 'you' form and added 'ta' prefix and sukun to make it present tense

steps.m: and thus you fall/perish (consequence of يَصُدَّنَّكَ - preventing you)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is the result of the previous action (being prevented from belief). The alif at the end is written in a special way (ىٰ) because it's at the end of the word.
This is a basic particle in Arabic that can have multiple functions depending on context

steps.m: and what (asking about تِلْكَ - that thing)
steps.n: Added و (and) to connect this question with what came before. This begins a conversation where Allah asks Moses about what he's holding.
The تِ is the basic feminine demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this/that'
Adding لْ changes it from pointing to something close to pointing to something far away

steps.m: that (feminine demonstrative pronoun pointing to something far)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it specifically for pointing to things that are far away
Added vowels (َ and ِ) and lengthened with ي to make the word mean 'right hand/side'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Added كَ to show it belongs to 'you' (when speaking to one male)

steps.m: in/with your right hand (answering وَمَا تِلْكَ - and what is that?)
steps.n: Added بِ meaning 'in/with'. This word answers the question 'what is that?' (وَمَا تِلْكَ) in the ayah
This is a proper name that doesn't come from an Arabic root. It comes from the Egyptian/Hebrew name meaning 'drawn out of water'
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: مُ (mu), وسَ (sa), ىٰ (aa)
Added يٰ at the start to show someone is calling out to Moses. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English
Added fatha vowels to make the basic past tense form. This word is part of Moses' response when asked about what's in his right hand.
The middle letter و changes to ا which is very common with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
This is a standalone pronoun used to refer to feminine singular subjects. In this ayah, it refers back to Moses's staff (عَصَا) when he answers Allah's question about what's in his right hand.
Added fatha vowels and the weak letter و changed to ا at the end to make the noun 'staff'

steps.m: my staff (object that Musa uses to lean on)
steps.n: Added ى with fatha to show this is 'my staff'. This staff is important because Musa uses it to lean on and for other purposes as mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: to support or lean on something

steps.n: The ت prefix and doubled middle letter ّ show that someone is doing the action to themselves, like saying 'I lean myself' instead of just 'I lean'
Adding أَ at the start shows 'I' am doing the action
The ُ at the end shows this is happening in the present time
The ا۟ at the end shows this is part of a list where Moses tells multiple things he does with his staff - he leans on it AND uses it for other things too
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning

steps.m: upon it/her (referring to عَصَاىَ - my staff)
steps.n: Added ها which means 'it/her' - here referring back to Moses's staff (عَصَاىَ) mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to shake or beat (like branches to make leaves fall)

steps.n: Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: I shake/beat (referring to Musa using his staff)
steps.n: Changed to first person present tense to show Musa speaking about what he does with his staff
Added conjunction to connect this action with the previous one (leaning on the staff)
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is a preposition meaning 'with'

steps.m: with it/her (referring to عَصَاىَ - my staff)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this refers back to Moses's staff (عَصَاىَ) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Basic preposition showing something is on top of, upon, or over something else
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the final form. In this ayah, it connects to 'my sheep' (غَنَمِى) to show what Musa was leaning upon.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun for a group of sheep
Added ِى at the end to show possession ('my'). The kasra (ِ) comes before the ى because this word follows the preposition عَلَىٰ (upon/on).
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Added ى with fatha at the end to show this means 'me', making the complete meaning 'and for me'
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ها suffix refers back to عَصَا (staff) which was just mentioned in the ayah. This creates a connection between the two words.

steps.m: to want something or need it

steps.n: Added مَ at the start to turn the action into a thing, like how 'write' becomes 'writing place' or 'writing tool'
Changed the word to show many needs or purposes instead of just one

steps.m: needs and purposes (as the main subject)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this word is doing something in the sentence

steps.m: to come after something or be behind it

steps.n: Root أخر becomes feminine word 'other' by adding ى at the end and changing vowels
Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb. This word is followed by أَلْقِهَا (throw it) as the spoken command.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Added أ at the start to show 'making someone/something do the action', and changed the ending because of spelling rules
Changed to command form by removing final ى and changing vowel to kasra

steps.m: throw it! (command to Moses about his staff)
steps.n: Added هَا to show the object (referring to the staff) being thrown
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the name Moses
Added يٰ at the beginning to show this is calling out to Moses. This is Allah speaking directly to Moses in this ayah.

steps.m: to throw, cast, or put down

steps.n: Changed root letters into past tense 'he' form by adding a-vowels (fatha)
Added أ at beginning to show more forceful or intentional action
Added ف to show this happened right after something else mentioned before
Added the small alif (ٰ) to show the long vowel sound clearly

steps.m: and then he threw it (referring to his staff)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show what was thrown (referring back to his staff mentioned earlier)
This is a special word that introduces something unexpected. In this ayah, it introduces the staff turning into a snake.

steps.m: and suddenly/and then (introduces the staff becoming a snake)
steps.n: The prefix فَ shows this happened right after he threw down the staff (فَأَلْقَىٰهَا)
This is the basic form of the pointing word that will become the feminine pronoun
Adding ي makes this specifically refer to a feminine subject (she/it)
Adding the vowel marks هِىَ helps with pronunciation. This pronoun refers to the staff (عصا) that turned into a snake, which is why it's feminine. It's the subject of تَسْعَىٰ (moves/crawls).
Root letters combined with vowels to make 'living' - doubled middle letter shows strength
Added feminine ending to create noun for snake, named for its lively movement
Added tanween dammah to show this is the subject doing the action of moving (تَسْعَىٰ) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the letters. The ي at the end becomes ى (alif maqsurah) because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added تَ at beginning to show feminine present tense. The middle letter gets sukun (ْ). This describes how the snake (حَيَّةٌ) is moving in the ayah.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's an alif maqsurah. This doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters ق-و-ل got vowels (َ) added to make قَوَلَ meaning 'he said'. This shows it's talking about something that already happened. Allah is the one saying this to Moses.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle. The word still means 'he said'.

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of something

steps.n: Changed into a command by removing the first letter and adding special vowels. This is telling one person to do something.

steps.m: take it! (command to one person to take the staff)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'it' referring to the staff (عصا) that Allah is commanding Moses to take. This connects to the previous ayah where the staff was mentioned.
Added fatha to show how the word is pronounced
Added لا (not) to و (and) to create the meaning 'and not'. This connects to the word تَخَفْ (fear) to say 'and do not fear'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed ending to show present tense
Changed from 'you are afraid' to 'do not fear!' by removing the last vowel and adding a stop mark (sukun) - this makes it a command to not be afraid
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The long vowel changes from ا to ي when making it present tense, like many similar verbs
Added نُ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action
Added سَ at the beginning to show this will happen in the future
Added هَا at the end to show what is being returned (it)

steps.m: to walk, move along a path

steps.n: Changed from a verb root meaning 'to walk' into a noun meaning 'way of walking' by adding the vowel mark 'i' in the middle
Added ة to make it refer to one specific way or manner

steps.m: its/her way (object of سَنُعِيدُ/we will return)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'her/its'. This word is what's being returned in the phrase 'we will return it'

steps.m: to come before or be first

steps.n: Changed root letters to make feminine word meaning 'first' by adding damma (ُ) and ending with alif (ى)
Added ٱلْ to make it specific (like 'the' in English)
Added superscript alif (ٰ) as a pronunciation guide. This word describes سِيرَتَهَا (its state/condition) in the ayah.

steps.m: to press together or bring close

steps.n: Added a damma (u sound) and doubled the middle letter to make it a command meaning 'press together!'
Added a hamza with damma at the start to make it stronger, and split the doubled 'm' into two letters with a sukun in between
Added 'and' at beginning to connect with previous command. This is Allah commanding Moses to press his hand to his side.
Added fatha to make it a noun meaning 'hand'. The final ي of the root is dropped in this form.
Added كَ to show it belongs to 'you', and fatha before it to show this is the object receiving the action of the command 'draw/press' (اضْمُمْ)
Basic preposition showing direction (in this ayah, it connects يَدَكَ (your hand) to جَنَاحِكَ (your side))
Added the small alif (ىٰ) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to lean or bend to one side
wing (because it bends to the side)
steps.n: Added the letter alif to make it into a noun meaning 'wing' - something that bends or moves to the side
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word belongs to or is connected to something
Added كَ at the end to show the wing belongs to you
Connected root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding تَ prefix for 'she'

steps.m: it will emerge (from يَدَكَ - your hand)
steps.n: Changed final vowel to sukun to show this is part of a sequence of events after وَٱضْمُمْ (and press). This word describes what will happen to يَدَكَ (your hand).
This pattern is used for colors in Arabic
Added اء at the end to make it feminine, like how we say 'white' for things that are feminine in Arabic

steps.m: white (feminine, in this sentence describing يَدَكَ - your hand)
steps.n: Added the final vowel mark and lengthened the alif to show it's describing 'your hand' in this sentence

steps.m: from (connecting غَيْرِ to what came before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects غَيْرِ (other than/without) to what came before it.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'other than'
Added kasra at the end because it's describing the word سُوٓءٍ (harm/evil) in the phrase 'without harm'
Root letters joined together with damma to make a noun meaning 'evil' instead of 'to do evil'
Added elongation mark (ٓ) over و to show long vowel sound
Added kasratin (ٍ) to show this is receiving the action of 'without' (مِنْ غَيْرِ) in the phrase 'without any harm'

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident
something that shows or makes clear
steps.n: The root letters change to make a noun meaning 'sign/miracle'. The middle letter becomes a long 'aa' sound, and the word starts with hamza.
Adding ة makes this a specific instance of something that shows or proves
The tanween fatha (ً) shows this is the object being acted upon by the verb تَخْرُجْ (it comes out)

steps.m: to come after, to be later

steps.n: Changed from root meaning 'later/after' to mean 'another' by adding feminine ending. Matches with the feminine word آيَةً (sign) it describes.
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى as a pronunciation guide

steps.m: to see/perceive with eyes or mind

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make someone see' instead of just 'to see'
Changes 'he shows' to 'we show' by adding نُ at start
Adds لِ (li-) to show purpose - 'in order to/so that'
Adds fatha (َ) at the end to complete the verb form
Adds كَ to show 'you' as the object - who is being shown to
This is a basic preposition that connects two parts by showing that something comes from somewhere else. Here it connects 'We show you' with 'Our signs', meaning 'We show you (from) Our signs'
Added sukun (ْ) on the ن to show the word ends here

steps.m: to show, appear, or be visible

steps.n: Root meaning 'to show' became a noun meaning 'a visible sign or miracle'
Changed from singular to plural form

steps.m: our signs (object of لِنُرِيَكَ/We will show you)
steps.n: Added 'our' to show these are Allah's signs. This word is what's being shown in the phrase 'We will show you.'
Changed to feminine superlative form showing this is the highest degree of greatness
Added ال to make it definite. This word describes 'Our signs' (ءَايٰتِنَا) in the ayah, indicating these are the greatest of Allah's signs.
Adding vowel marks to make it a command form
Added hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) at the beginning to help pronunciation. This word is connected to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh) in this ayah through إِلَىٰ (to) which comes after it.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects the command 'go' with its destination 'Pharaoh'.
Added the small alif (ىٰ) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a proper noun referring to the ruler of ancient Egypt. In this ayah, he is the one being addressed by the command 'go to'.
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of the preposition إِلَىٰ (to) in the phrase 'go to Pharaoh'
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' in English. It's used at the start of the statement about Pharaoh.
Added هُ (with extension mark ۥ) at the end to show we're talking about 'him' (Pharaoh). This connects back to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past
The final ي with fatha changes to ى with alif khanjariya (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed from basic root 'say' to 'he said' by adding vowel marks. Shows this is past tense and starts Musa's prayer.
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Doubling the ب makes the meaning stronger and more intense

steps.m: O my Lord! (calling upon the Lord)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end makes it into a way of calling out to your Lord
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to command form by adding alif at start and sukun at end. This is Moses asking Allah to expand his chest (make him more confident/capable)
This is a basic preposition that shows something is 'for' someone
Adding ى at the end shows this belongs to or relates to me. This word connects to صَدْرِى (my chest) to show I am asking about my own chest.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun. This word is what's being expanded in the phrase 'expand my chest'.
Adding ى with kasra (ِ) shows this chest belongs to me. In the phrase 'expand my chest', this is what's being expanded.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Doubling the س makes the meaning more intense - from 'made easy' to 'made very easy'

steps.m: and make (it) very easy! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ regarding أَمْرِى)
steps.n: Changed from past tense 'made easy' to a command 'make easy!' Also added 'wa' (and) at start. This is asking Allah to make something easy, specifically 'my affair' mentioned after it
Added kasra to show this word connects to what comes after it

steps.m: for me (connected to أَمْرِى - my matter)
steps.n: Added ى with a long vowel mark to show this means 'me'. This word connects to أَمْرِى (my matter) to say 'for me my matter'
Changed from basic root letters to a noun by adding vowel marks - now means the actual matter/affair instead of the action of commanding
Added kasra to show this word will be possessed by something

steps.m: my matter/affair (object of يَسِّرْ - make easy)
steps.n: Added ى to show this is my matter/affair. This is what is being made easy in the phrase 'make easy for me my matter'

steps.m: to untie or loosen something that is bound

steps.n: Added the vowels that make this a command form. The ُ (damma) and ْ (sukun) show this is telling someone to do something.
Added ٱ at start to make it a proper command form that's easier to say

steps.m: and untie! (command to untie the knot from لِّسَانِى my tongue)
steps.n: Added وَ (wa) at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it. This is part of Moses's prayer asking Allah to untie the knot from his tongue.
Changed the word from an action (tying) into a thing (knot) by adding specific vowel patterns and ة at the end

steps.m: a knot (that Moses wants untied from his tongue)
steps.n: Adding ً shows this is the thing being acted upon by the verb احْلُلْ (untie). In this ayah, Moses is asking Allah to untie a knot from his tongue that makes it hard for him to speak clearly.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a sukun, making the م sound stronger for easier pronunciation
Added vowels and alif to make the noun pattern for body parts
Added ي at the end to show possession (my)
The ل gets a shadda because it comes after the word مِن. The ى at the end is just a different way to write ي - both mean 'my'.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense (he understood) to present tense (he understands) by adding يَ at the start. The word shows what is being understood - قَوْلِى (my words).
Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' as the ones doing the understanding
Changed from action to noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter

steps.m: my speech (object of يَفْقَهُوا - they understand)
steps.n: Added ي with kasra to show possession. This word is what is being understood in the phrase 'they understand my speech'
Adding fathas creates the basic past tense active verb form
Changed to command form by adding اِ at start and sukuns (ْ) on middle and end

steps.m: and make!/and appoint! (for لِّى - for me)
steps.n: Adding وَ at start connects this to previous phrase. This word is connected to لِّى (for me) which comes after it, showing Musa asking Allah to appoint for him.
Added kasra to show it's a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'
Added ي at the end to show this means 'for/to me' specifically
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning, and changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation
Changes from action to person who does the action
The ًا ending shows this is the object of اجْعَل (make/appoint). Moses is asking Allah to make/appoint Aaron as his helper.
This is a connecting word that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects 'minister' (وَزِيرًا) with 'my family' (أَهْلِى)
The kasra and sukun marks are added to show how to pronounce 'from' correctly - no shadda is present or needed here
The letter ن merges with the next letter, shown by the shadda mark

steps.m: to belong to a family, to be settled

steps.n: Root letters أ-ه-ل got short vowels (َ and ْ) to make the basic noun meaning 'family'
Added the suffix ي with kasra to show possession (my)
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew that refers to Prophet Moses's brother. The word أَخِى (my brother) after it in the ayah confirms this relationship.
The fatha at the end shows that Aaron is being described by the word أَخِى (my brother) that comes after it
The root letters أ-خ-و become the simple word 'brother' (أَخ) by dropping the و at the end and adding a fatha mark
Added kasra to show this word is owned by someone
Added ى at the end to show it means 'my brother'. This word belongs to Harun (هٰرُونَ) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to command form by adding shadda and changing vowels to 'u' sound
Changed to command form by adding alif at start and changing vowels - now means 'you make strong!'
Basic pronoun form referring back to the word أَزْرِى (my strength) in this ayah

steps.m: with/by him/it (referring to أَزْرِى - my strength)
steps.n: Preposition بِ (with/by) added to pronoun

steps.m: with/by him/it (referring to أَزْرِى - my strength)
steps.n: Added pronunciation helpers ۦٓ which don't change the meaning
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make the word pronounceable
Added ى at the end to show the strength belongs to me. This word is the object that is being strengthened in the phrase 'strengthen my strength'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adding أ (hamza) at the start changes the meaning from 'to share' to 'to make someone/something a partner' - like turning 'share' into 'make share'
Changed the verb into a command form by changing the vowels and adding sukun at the end
Added 'and' to connect this command with what came before

steps.m: and make him a partner! (in أَمْرِى my matter)
steps.n: The هُ (hu) at the end means 'him' - showing who is being made a partner
Basic preposition that shows location or connection. Here it connects 'make him share' (أَشْرِكْهُ) with 'my matter/affair' (أَمْرِى)
Added kasra (ِ) vowel mark under ف to show how it's pronounced
Changed the final ي to ى with a small alif (ٓ) above it. This is just a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to order, command, or be in charge of something

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'command' into the noun 'matter/affair' by adding marks (fatha-sukun pattern)

steps.m: my matter/affair (object of أَشْرِكْهُ - include him in)
steps.n: Added the first person possessive pronoun ي with kasra before it to show this is 'my matter'. This word is what is being shared/included according to the verb أَشْرِكْهُ (include him) in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that shows why something happens

steps.m: so that (introduces نُسَبِّحَكَ - we glorify You)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) on ك and sukun (ْ) on ي to complete the word. This word introduces the purpose of an action - here it shows why something is done: 'so that we may glorify You.'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger - to glorify extensively or repeatedly

steps.m: we glorify intensely (acted on by كَىْ)
steps.n: The نُ at the start shows 'we' are doing the action. The kasra (ِ) appears because the word follows كَىْ (so that/in order to)

steps.m: we glorify you intensely (acted on by كَىْ)
steps.n: The كَ shows who is being glorified (you)
This pattern makes the word into an adjective meaning 'much' or 'many'
Root letters got vowels to make it mean 'became much/many'
Added ي to make the quality stronger

steps.m: abundantly (describing how نُسَبِّحَ/we glorify is done)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this describes how the action is done. It tells us how much we glorify.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from past tense 'he mentioned' to command form 'mention!'
Adding نَ at the beginning shows 'we' are doing the action
Adding ك at the end shows you are the one being mentioned/remembered
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to the previous phrase with 'and'
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to make it an adjective describing abundance

steps.m: much (describing how we remember/mention in وَنَذْكُرَكَ)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this word describes how the action is done in the phrase 'and we remember/mention you much'
This is a special emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding an exclamation mark in English
Added كَ at the end to show this is talking about 'you'. This word connects to كُنتَ (you were) that comes after it.
Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'being' by adding vowels
The weak letter و drops out between two consonants
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did this action in the past. This word is the subject of إِنَّكَ (indeed you)
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or connects one thing to another
Added نَا which means 'us' when attached to a preposition. In this ayah, بِنَا is connected to بَصِيرًا (All-Seeing) to show who Allah sees.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters and changed vowels to show someone who does this action extensively

steps.m: one who sees deeply/thoroughly (as a description of كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this word is describing كُنتَ (you were) in the ayah
Basic root ق-و-ل became past tense 'he said' by adding the vowels. This word connects to 'you were given' later in the verse.
The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas - this is a common change with weak letters

steps.m: certainly (emphasizing أُوتِيتَ - you have been given)
steps.n: This is a particle that adds certainty to the verb that follows it. Here it emphasizes that Musa was definitely given what he asked for.
Changed to passive form ('was' given) by adding ُ and ِ marks. The ت in the root changed to و, which is common when making this type of word.

steps.m: you were given (what يٰمُوسَىٰ asked for)
steps.n: Added ت to show this happened to 'you' (Moses) in the past. This relates to the following word سُؤْلَكَ (your request) in the ayah.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma (ُ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter. The hamza (ء) becomes (ؤ) when it has a sukun after damma.
Added fatha (َ) to show this word is receiving the action of being given (أُوتِيتَ - you were given)

steps.m: your request (that was given to you)
steps.n: Added كَ to show the request belongs to the person being addressed (Moses). This word is what was given (object of أُوتِيتَ - you were given)
This is a proper name that doesn't come from an Arabic root. It's the Arabic version of the name Moses.
Added يٰ at the start to show someone is being called or addressed directly. This is used when Allah is calling out to Moses.
This is a common particle used to emphasize that something definitely happened in the past, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
The لَ adds even more emphasis, making the statement stronger

steps.m: and indeed definitely (connects to مَنَنَّا - We bestowed favor)
steps.n: The وَ connects this phrase to what comes after it (مَنَنَّا - We bestowed favor), showing these events are connected

steps.m: to give a gift or favor
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of showing kindness or favor to someone
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
The doubled ن shows this was a special or important gift
Adding ا at the end changes it from 'he gave' to 'we gave'

steps.m: upon/on (preposition showing something happening to or affecting something else)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows something happening to or affecting something else, like saying 'The book is on the table' in English
The كَ at the end means 'you' (when talking to one male person)
This root is about something happening or passing by
Adding the last fatha makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he passed by'
Changed the verb to a noun and added ة to show one single time something happened

steps.m: a time, an instance (of أُخْرَىٰٓ - another)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is indefinite (like 'a' in English). This word is describing أُخْرَىٰٓ (another) in the ayah.
Adding damma and sukun to create the base trilateral form
Adding ى at the end makes this word feminine, meaning 'another' or 'other'
The alif at the end (ى) is written with a small mark above to show it's pronounced differently
This is a time particle that connects two events, showing when the second event happened in relation to the first. In this ayah, it introduces when something was revealed.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to show causing something to happen. The final ى is because this is a weak verb.
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
The alif at the end makes the 'aa' sound longer when stopping on this word
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects أَوْحَيْنَآ (we revealed) to أُمِّكَ (your mother), showing who received the revelation.
Added alif maksura (ى with two dots becomes ى without dots) at the end. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word. This is a pronunciation mark that shows you should stretch the sound a little when reading.

steps.m: to be a mother, to lead as a source or origin

steps.n: The ُ (damma) makes it into the noun 'mother'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something after it
Added كَ at the end to show this means 'your mother'
This word introduces what is being revealed, connecting it to the previous phrase about revelation to your mother
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he revealed' to 'being revealed to' by adding يُ at start and changing end to ى. Like changing from active to passive voice in English.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's an elongated alif sound
Added the small high mark (ٓ) to show this is the final word form
This is a basic connecting word that introduces what comes next, like saying 'that' in English. It connects to the command that follows (ٱقْذِفِيهِ - cast him)
The small i-sound (ِ) is added because this word connects to a command word after it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense to a command by adding alif and changing vowels
Added ي with kasra to make it a feminine command

steps.m: throw him! (to a female) (into التَّابُوتِ - the chest)
steps.n: Added هِ meaning 'him' to show what is being thrown. Also changed initial alif to alif wasl since it's followed by sukun. This word relates to التَّابُوتِ (the chest) which is where the throwing is directed.
This is a preposition that shows where something happens. In this ayah, it shows where Musa's mother should place him - in the box/ark.
This is a borrowed word that became part of Arabic vocabulary. Adding vowel marks to show pronunciation.
Added the definite article ال which causes the first letter to get a shadda (doubling mark)
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word في (in/into) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Changed from 'he threw' to 'throw!' by adding 'i' at start and changing middle vowel - makes it a command
Added فَ (so/then) to connect this command with what came before
Added ي to show this command is for a female (in this case, Moses's mother)
Added هِ to show who is being thrown (referring to baby Moses)
This is a basic word that shows where or into what something happens. Here it connects 'throw him' with 'the chest'.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This doesn't change the meaning, just the spelling.
The root يَمم becomes ٱلْيَمّ (the sea) by adding ٱلْ (the) and doubling the م with a shaddah
The kasra shows this word comes after the word فِي (in) in the ayah
Added يُ at start to show 'he' does the action, with ُ showing it's happening now/future
Added لِ at start to show 'in order to' or 'so that'
Changed to command form and added هِ (hi) at end to show 'it' - what's being thrown
Added فَ to show this happens as a result of something before it

steps.m: sea or large body of water

steps.n: Root letters joined together with a shadda (double letter) in the middle to make the word for 'sea'
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific sea

steps.m: the sea (subject of يَأْخُذْهُ/will take him)
steps.n: Added damma to show this word is the subject doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: coast, shore (literally: that which is worn away by water)
steps.n: Adds ا after first letter to make it a noun meaning 'place that experiences the action'
Added ال (the) and doubled the س with shadda to show it's a specific shore
Added بِ (by/at) at the beginning and changed اَل to ٱل because it comes after a preposition
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the preposition بِ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The sukun at the end shows this is in a special form (jussive) because it comes after فَلْ meaning 'then let'

steps.m: he takes/will take him (object of فَلْيُلْقِهِ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being taken. This connects to the previous command 'then let the river cast him'

steps.m: to be hostile or cross boundaries

steps.n: The pattern makes it into a noun describing someone with this quality
Added double damma to show this is describing someone in general terms. In this ayah, it's describing an enemy of Allah.
Basic combination of preposition ل (to/for) with pronoun ي (me)

steps.m: to/for me (emphasizing the connection to عَدُوٌّ)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل for emphasis and changed ي to ى. This word connects to عَدُوٌّ (enemy) to show 'an enemy to me'

steps.m: to be hostile or exceed limits

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the final letter to create a noun meaning someone who does the action. This word is connected to يأخذ (takes/receives) in the ayah.
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is any enemy, not a specific one
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Adding shadda (ّ) to lam makes it emphatic, showing certainty
Adding هُ at the end shows who is being referred to (him)
The ۥ mark is just a pronunciation helper that shows the هُ should be pronounced clearly
Adding hamza prefix makes it mean 'cause to throw'. The final ى is because it's a weak verb.
Adding و at the beginning connects this action with what came before it
Adding تُ at the end shows that 'I' did this action in the past. The ي comes back before the تُ because it's a weak verb.
Starting with the basic preposition عَلَى which shows something being on top of or upon something else
The ى changes to يْ (with sukun) to prepare for adding a suffix
Added كَ at the end to show this affects 'you' (one male person)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
When two same letters come together, they combine with a shadda mark
Adding مَ at start turns verb into noun showing where action happens
Adding ة turns it into the abstract concept of love
The tanween (ً) shows this is the object receiving the action of 'placing' in the verse
Starting with the basic preposition meaning 'from'
The ن doubled and ي attached at end to make it mean 'from me'
Added a shadda on the first letter because it comes after another word ending in a similar sound. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes from 'he created' to 'being created' by adding تُ prefix and changing vowels to show something is receiving the action

steps.m: so that you may be created
steps.n: Adds لِ to show purpose - this explains why something is happening

steps.m: and so that you may be created (under My supervision - عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ)
steps.n: Adds وَ to connect this phrase with the previous one. This whole phrase explains why Allah put love for Musa in people's hearts - so that he would be raised under Allah's supervision.
This is a primary preposition that doesn't derive from a root. It's an invariable particle.
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation
Added small alif (ٰ) above the alif maksura to show it's a long vowel. This word connects to عَيْنِىٓ (my eye) in the ayah.

steps.m: eye, spring, or essence of something

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to create the basic noun form meaning 'eye'
Added kasra to show this eye belongs to someone
Added ى to show the eye belongs to me
Added a long ي to emphasize the 'my' sound
This is a time particle that connects to the verb تَمْشِىٓ (she walked) to show when something happened in the past. It helps tell the story about when Musa's sister was walking.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added ي at end to show this is a complete present tense verb. The ي shows it's singular.
Changes the base word into feminine form by adding vowel marks and feminine ت
Adds ُ at the end to show this is the subject (the sister is doing something)
Adding كَ at the end means 'your sister' - shows the sister belongs to you
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changes from 'he said' in past to 'you say' in present - adds تَ at start to show 'you'

steps.m: and then you say/speak (to your sister)
steps.n: The فَ (fa) at the start means 'and then/so', showing what happens next in the story

steps.m: do/does (asking أُخْتُكَ if she can guide)
steps.n: This is a question word that turns a statement into a yes/no question, like adding 'do' or 'does' at the start of an English question. Here it's used by the sister to ask if she can guide them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Combined the repeated letters with shadda to show emphasis
Added hamza with fatha at start and damma on middle letter to show 'I' am doing the action

steps.m: I guide you all (to مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being guided. This connects to the next phrase 'who will take care of him'

steps.m: upon (connecting أُخْتُكَ 'your sister' to what follows)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects 'your sister' to what follows.

steps.m: upon (connecting أُخْتُكَ 'your sister' to what follows)
steps.n: Added an alif mark (ٰ) to show this is a long 'aa' sound at the end. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: who/whoever (introduces the person who يَكْفُلُهُۥ - would take care of him)
steps.n: This is a question word used to ask about people. Here it introduces the person who would take care of baby Musa. The word يَكْفُلُهُۥ (would take care of him) directly follows and describes what this person would do.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed from past to present by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: he cares for him (referring to baby Moses)
steps.n: Added هُۥ to show who is being cared for. In this ayah, it refers to baby Moses who needs someone to take care of him.
Added vowels to make 'he returned' in the past tense
Added فَ (fa-) meaning 'so/then' to show this action follows from previous events mentioned in the ayah
Added نٰ (naa) to show 'we' as the doers. This refers to Allah using the royal 'We'

steps.m: so We returned you (to your mother)
steps.n: Added كَ (ka) meaning 'you' as the object - the person being returned. From context, this refers to baby Moses being returned to his mother.
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. Here it connects 'your sister' to 'your mother' in the story.
Added alif maksura (ى with two dots becomes ى without dots) at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the last letter. This mark shows that the sound is stretched slightly when reading, but doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be a source or origin

steps.n: Root أمم becomes أُمّ (mother), then kasra shows it belongs to something after it
Added كَ to show this is your mother. This word comes after إِلَىٰٓ (to) in the ayah.
This is a connecting word that shows why something happens. Here it connects 'she walked' with 'her eye would be comforted', showing that the walking happened for this purpose.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb
The doubled ر (shaddah) emphasizes the state of being settled/content

steps.m: she settles/becomes content (so that عَيْنُهَا her eye)
steps.n: Adding تَ shows this is happening to 'her eye' (عَيْنُهَا) in the present tense. In Arabic, when someone's eye 'becomes cool/settled', it means they become happy or content.

steps.m: to see or watch with the eye

steps.n: The root becomes a noun meaning the physical eye. This word is the subject of تَقَرَّ (becomes cool/content), forming the common Arabic expression for being happy or satisfied.
Adds ُ to show this noun is doing the action in the sentence
Adds هَا meaning 'her' to show whose eye it is. This forms part of the expression تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا meaning 'her eye becomes cool' (she becomes happy/content).
This is a negative particle that prevents the following verb تَحْزَنَ (be sad) from happening
Added وَ (and) to connect this phrase with what came before it, showing these events happened one after another
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: that you become sad (connected to كَىْ meaning 'so that')
steps.n: Added تَ at the start to change 'he' to 'you', and made the middle letter quiet (with sukun). This happens because كَىْ (so that) comes before it.
Basic past tense form of the verb
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did the action

steps.m: to breathe out, to be valuable
soul/self (acted on by قَتَلْتَ - you killed)
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'soul' or 'self'. This is what is being killed in the sentence.
Added ًا to show this is what's being acted upon - the person that was killed. Like saying 'you killed someone' where 'someone' is the thing being acted on.
