The doubled middle letter (ّ) shows this is done with extra force or emphasis
Added نا to show 'we' are doing the action. This shows Allah speaking in the royal 'We'
كَ shows that Moses is the one being saved. The alif (ٰ) before كَ is added to help with pronunciation

steps.m: so we saved you (connected to previous events)
steps.n: فَ connects this to what happened before - Allah is listing the favors He did for Moses
This is the basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word ٱلْغَمِّ (distress). This combination means 'from the distress'

steps.m: to cover completely or overwhelm with distress

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is intense distress that completely overwhelms someone

steps.m: the distress (that you were saved from)
steps.n: Adding ال shows this refers to a specific distress - in this case, the one mentioned in the ayah that Allah saved Moses from
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Doubled middle letter to show intensity of testing through trials
Added ا to show 'we' did the testing

steps.m: we tested you through many trials
steps.n: Added ك to show Moses was the one being tested through various trials in his life

steps.m: and we tested you through many trials (connected to فَرَجَعْنٰكَ - 'we returned you')
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect this testing with the previous mention of returning Moses to his mother

steps.m: to test through trials or hardship

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to test' to plural noun meaning 'trials'

steps.m: with much testing (describing how Allah tested Moses)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how the testing was done, relating to وَفَتَنَّاكَ (and We tested you)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb. The fatha-kasra-fatha pattern is common for verbs describing states that last for some time.
Changed 'he' to 'you' by adding تَ at the end. This is addressing Moses directly about his time in Madyan.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after the previous events mentioned (being saved from distress). This connects it to the previous ayah.
Root س-ن-و means passing time, adding ة makes it mean one specific year
Changed the vowels to kasra (ِ) and added ين at the end to show multiple years
Added a fatha (َ) at the end because this word shows how long 'you remained' - it answers 'for how many years?'
This is a basic preposition showing location or being inside something. Here it shows Moses was staying in the city of Madyan.
The ى with ٓ (alif) is just a spelling variation of ي that doesn't change the meaning. It's used when the word pauses here in recitation.
Added fatha and sukun to make the basic noun form meaning 'people/inhabitants'
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or is connected to Madyan
Forms the name of the ancient city Madyan by adding vowels and the letter ي. This was the city where Prophet Musa fled to after leaving Egypt.

steps.m: Madyan (name of an ancient city where Prophet Musa stayed)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word's role in the sentence

steps.m: then (connecting what happened after وَفَتَنَّاكَ فُتُونًا فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ)
steps.n: This word connects events in sequence, showing that one thing happened after another. Here it connects Moses's time in Madyan with his return according to Allah's plan.
The middle weak letter changes to hamza with kasra due to phonetic rules
Added تَ to show 'you' (masculine singular) did the action
This is the basic form meaning something being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition showing something is on top of or over something else
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is connecting to the word مَن (who) that follows it in the ayah
Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb form
Changed from a past tense verb (he determined) to a noun (appointed time/decree) with tanwin, making it indefinite (an appointed time)
Added vowel marks to properly pronounce the name Moses. The ىٰ at the end with alif khanjariya (small alif above) is a special way of writing this name.
Added يٰ at the beginning to show we are calling out to Moses. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ط between first two letters to show doing something for oneself. The ت changes to ط after ص for easier pronunciation.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past
Added كَ at the end to show you are receiving the action (like saying 'you' in 'I chose you')

steps.m: And I specially chose you (for لِنَفْسِى myself)
steps.n: Added وَ at start to mean 'and'. The اِ changes to ٱ after وَ for easier pronunciation.
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second to make the noun 'self'
Added kasra to show this word is owned by something
Added لِ at beginning to show this is 'for' or 'to' something
Added ى at the end to show this means 'my'
Added sukun-fatha-sukun pattern that makes a basic command for one person
Added helper alif at start to make the command easier to pronounce. This word is addressed to Moses (مُوسَى) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'you'. It starts with hamza (أ) and nun (ن) with sukun.

steps.m: you (when talking to one male) (subject of اذْهَبْ - go)
steps.n: These are actually two different words - أَنْ is 'that' while أَنتَ is 'you'. They're not related by transformation. The pronoun أَنتَ is used here as the subject being told to 'go' (اذْهَبْ).

steps.m: to be a brother, to act as a brother

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a noun meaning 'brother'
Added كَ at the end to show it's your brother. This word joins with أنت (you) as subjects of اذْهَبْ (go).
Added وَ (and) to connect this word with أنت (you), making them both subjects of اذْهَبْ (go).

steps.m: to show evidence or make clear

steps.n: Root letters combine with long 'aa' sound to make the noun meaning 'sign'
Changes singular 'sign' to plural 'signs'
Adds 'my' to show these are Allah's signs being referred to

steps.m: with my signs (used with ٱذْهَبْ - go forth)
steps.n: Adds 'with' to show these signs are tools/means for the action of 'going forth' mentioned in the ayah
This is a negative particle that makes something not happen. In this ayah, it's telling someone not to do something.
The وَ connects this negative command to what came before it. In this ayah, it's connecting two instructions: go with My signs AND don't weaken in remembering Me.
Connected the letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Changed the first letter to ت to show 'you' are doing the action

steps.m: you both weaken (addressing Moses and Aaron)
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show this is talking about two people (Moses and Aaron) in this ayah
This is a standalone preposition particle that doesn't derive from a root. It indicates location or containment.
Added kasra (ِ) vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced
Changed the final ي to ى which is a common spelling variation at the end of words
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning 'remembrance'
Added ى at the end to show it belongs to me (first person possession)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he went' to a command 'go!' by adding alif at start and making the word shorter and sharper sounding
Added آ at the end to show this command is for two people (Moses and Aaron in this case)
This is a basic preposition that shows direction. In this ayah, it connects 'go!' (ٱذْهَبَآ) with 'Pharaoh' (فِرْعَوْنَ), showing where to go.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under hamza and fatha (َ) over lam.
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ya to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound at the end.
Adding the basic vowel marks to properly pronounce the name: kasra on ف, sukun on ر, and connecting و with ن

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object of اذْهَبَا إِلَى - go to)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this is the object being acted upon - in this case, the one being 'gone to' in the phrase 'go to Pharaoh'
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English. Here it emphasizes that Pharaoh really did go beyond limits (طَغَىٰ)
Added هُۥ (hu) at the end to mean 'he/him', referring back to Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ) mentioned earlier. The small و (ۥ) after ه is just to help with pronunciation.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he transgressed (subject of إِنَّهُۥ - indeed he)
steps.n: The final ي changes to ى with alif above it because it's at the end of the word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he said' to a command 'say!' by changing the middle vowel from 'aa' to 'uu'
Added alif at the end to make it a command for two people

steps.m: and say! / so say! (command to two people)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action follows from something before it
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of this preposition

steps.m: to/for him (connected to فَقُولَا - and say you two)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (him) to show who the action is directed towards. This word connects to فَقُولَا (and say you two) to show who they should speak to.

steps.m: to/for him (connected to فَقُولَا - and say you two)
steps.n: Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Changed from basic root meaning 'speak' to noun form meaning 'speech' by adding vowels
The ًا at the end shows this is the thing being said in the command 'say to him' (فَقُولَا لَهُ). The next word لَّيِّنًا tells us this should be a gentle speech.
Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make the root letters spell out 'gentle/soft'
The doubled ي with shadda makes the meaning stronger
Added shadda on first letter for extra emphasis

steps.m: very gently/softly (as the manner of speaking)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this describes how to speak (related to قَوْلًا - 'speech' in this ayah)
Adding emphasis to show importance of what follows
Adding the pronoun 'he' to show who might do the action. This connects to Pharaoh who is being spoken about in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more intensive
Adding ت at start shows the person is doing the action to themselves
Adding يَ at start and ُ at end makes it present/future tense
This is a connecting word that joins two possibilities: 'he may remember' or 'he may fear'. In this ayah, it connects two hoped-for outcomes when speaking gently to Pharaoh.

steps.m: to fear or be afraid of something

steps.n: Basic past tense verb showing the feeling of fear
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The final ي of the root changes to ى because it's at the end of a present tense verb.
Added the small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long vowel. This doesn't change the meaning, just helps with pronunciation.
Adding the basic past tense vowel pattern

steps.m: they both said (to رَبَّنَا - our Lord)
steps.n: The root قَوَلَ changes to قَالَ (said) and adds ا at the end to show two people speaking
Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to show this is someone who nurtures perfectly

steps.m: our Lord (being addressed by قَالَا - they both said)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show we're talking about our Lord. This is what's being said in the phrase 'they both said' (قَالَا)

steps.m: Oh our Lord! (calling out to Him)
steps.n: The extra alif (آ) at the end shows we're calling out to our Lord, like saying 'Oh our Lord!'
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It makes the sentence stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English.

steps.m: indeed we (subject of نَخَافُ - we fear)
steps.n: نا at the end means 'we' when attached to this particle
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters. The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas.

steps.m: we fear (of أَن يَفْرُطَ - that he will exceed)
steps.n: Added نَ at the start to mean 'we' and ُ at the end to show it's happening now. This word shows what Musa and Harun were afraid of, which is explained in the next part of the ayah (أَن يَفْرُطَ - that he will exceed).

steps.m: that (connects to يَفْرُطَ - he will exceed)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces what someone fears, thinks, or expects might happen. Here it connects to the verb يَفْرُطَ (he will exceed) to show what Musa and Harun were afraid of.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present/future tense. Changed vowels to show potential action (that Musa and Harun fear might happen). This connects to عَلَيْنَآ (against us) in the ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is on or against something else
The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a suffix - this is just a spelling change

steps.m: upon us (connected to نَخَافُ - we fear)
steps.n: Added نَا which means 'us' to show who is being acted upon. This word connects to نَخَافُ (we fear) in the ayah, showing what they fear about.

This word connects two possibilities that Moses and Aaron were worried about: that Pharaoh might either act hastily against them (يَفْرُطَ) or exceed bounds/become tyrannical (يَطْغَىٰ). It shows these are two separate but related concerns.

steps.m: that (connects to يَفْرُطَ - he will exceed)
steps.n: This word connects two ideas in the sentence. Here it shows what Musa and Harun are afraid of - that Pharaoh might exceed bounds against them. It makes the next verb (يَفْرُطَ) into 'will exceed' instead of just 'exceeds'.

steps.m: to cross limits, to go too far
steps.n: The root carries the idea of going beyond limits in a negative way
Root letters connected with fathas to make past tense verb 'he'. The ي changes to ى since it's at the end.

steps.m: he goes too far (in the context of فَيَفْرُطَ - so that he might exceed)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense, and put sukun on ط to make it easier to pronounce. This word shows what Musa and Harun were worried Pharaoh might do.
Added small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long vowel sound at the end

steps.m: he said (subject of لَا تَخَافَآ - do not fear)
steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This is what Allah said to Moses and Aaron when they were afraid.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it (تَخَافَآ - you two fear) into its opposite meaning (do not fear)
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا when used in a word.
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' and changed the last vowel to ُ to show present tense
Added ا at the end to show two people doing the action
Adding لا makes it 'do not' - tells two people not to do something
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to مَعَكُمَآ - with you two)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. This connects to the next words مَعَكُمَآ (with you two) to say 'indeed I am with you two'
This is a preposition meaning 'with' - it doesn't come from a root since it's a basic word

steps.m: with you two (addressed in قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ - He said: Do not fear)
steps.n: Added كُمَا which shows this is addressing exactly two people. This matches with the previous words قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ (He said: Do not fear) which are also addressing two people.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed from past to present tense by adding sukun on first letter and damma at end. The 'ya' at the start (meaning 'he') is missing in this example but would normally be there

steps.m: I hear/listen (present tense, in this ayah showing Allah speaking)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to show first person (I)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make someone see' instead of just 'to see'
Added conjunction و to connect this with the previous word أَسْمَعُ (I hear)

steps.m: and I see (connected to أَسْمَعُ - I hear)
steps.n: Added alif maksura at the end to show the final weak letter
Changed from basic verb 'to come' into a command by dropping letters and adding special marks
Added ف with fatha at beginning to show sequence
Added alif at end to make it a command for two people

steps.m: so you two go to him! (command to Moses and Aaron about Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added هُ at end to show who they should go to (Pharaoh)

steps.m: You two speak! (command for two people)
steps.n: The ا at the end shows we're talking to exactly two people
Added فَ at beginning to connect it with the previous command (فَأْتِيَاهُ - and go to him)
Adding shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphatic 'indeed', making the statement stronger
Adding ا at the end makes this refer to 'we', showing that Moses and Aaron are speaking together to Pharaoh
The و makes it mean someone who does this action a lot
Adding ا at the end makes it refer to two instead of one
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and cares for others perfectly and completely
The kasra ending shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
Adding كَ shows this Lord belongs to you (the one being spoken to). In this ayah, Moses and Aaron are being told to say they are messengers of Pharaoh's Lord.

steps.m: to send or deliver something from one place/person to another

steps.n: Adding these vowel marks changes it from a basic root into a command form
Added أَ at beginning to make the command clearer and easier to pronounce
Added فَ to connect this command with the previous statement, showing it follows from what was said before
This is a preposition meaning 'with' - it has no root since it's a basic particle
Added نَا at the end to show who is being sent with. This word connects to the command 'send' (أَرْسِلْ) in the ayah, showing that the Children of Israel should be sent 'with us'.

steps.m: to build, establish, or create offspring

steps.n: Changed from root letters to past tense verb 'he built' by adding vowel marks
These are actually two different words - not a transformation. 'Bana' means to build, while 'bani' means children/offspring
The vowel pattern shows this is a plural noun meaning 'children of'
This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew meaning 'one who struggles with God' - referring to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب). It has no Arabic root.

steps.m: Israel (the prophet Jacob, as the object of بَنِى/children of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of بَنِى (children of) in the phrase بَنِى إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ (Children of Israel)
This is a negative particle that stops something from happening. Here it tells Musa and Harun not to do the action in the next word (punish/torture).
Adding وَ connects this command to the previous statements. It shows this is another part of what Musa and Harun should say to Pharaoh.
Added shadda to show intensive punishment
Changes from past to present tense and adds person marker
Makes the verb jussive (like a command or condition)

steps.m: you punish them intensively (in context of فَقُولَآ - 'and you both say')
steps.n: Added pronoun 'them' as object of punishment. This is part of what Moses and Aaron are commanded to say to Pharaoh.

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizing جِئْنٰكَ - we have come to you)
steps.n: This is a particle that adds emphasis to the verb that follows it (جِئْنٰكَ). It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' before a statement in English to make it stronger.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The alif changes to hamza with sukun due to joining with the following suffix
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Added ك to show the action was directed 'to you', with alif changing to alif maqsurah
Forms a noun meaning 'sign' or 'miracle' by adding ة at the end. The آ is a long version of أ.
Adds بِ at beginning to show this is being used as evidence or proof (this sign is being brought as proof to Pharaoh)

steps.m: with a sign/miracle (from رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Adds tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite (any sign) and is connected to the words that follow (from your Lord). This word is connected to جِئْنٰكَ (we came to you) earlier in the sentence.
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or someone
Added a shadda (doubling mark) on م because it comes after a word ending in a vowel. This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Doubling the ب shows intensity of nurturing/caring

steps.m: O my Lord! (calling upon one's Lord)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end makes it a call/address to one's Lord
Added كَ at the end to change from 'my Lord' to 'your Lord', and doubled the ر at the start
Changes the action into the resulting state or condition
Added 'the' and made it the subject with the ُ ending. The long alif (ٰ) is just a writing variation.

steps.m: and the peace/safety (greeting in response to following guidance)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this greeting with what comes after. This is part of a greeting formula meaning 'peace be upon those who follow guidance.'
This is a preposition that comes from the concept of being high or above something
Adding ى at the end makes it work as a preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is connected to the word مَنِ (whoever) that follows it, making the phrase mean 'upon whoever'
This is a relative pronoun that introduces a conditional clause. It refers to any person who does the action that follows.
Added kasra at the end because it's followed by a word starting with a hamza (اتَّبَعَ). This is just for easier pronunciation.

steps.m: to follow or go after something/someone

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added alif at start and doubled the ت to show intensive following
Changed initial alif to waṣla form since it's just a pronunciation helper
Changes from verb 'to guide' into the noun 'guidance'
Adding ال makes it refer to specific guidance rather than guidance in general
Added a long vowel mark (ٰ) and a small alif (ٓ) to show proper pronunciation
This is an emphasis particle that begins sentences to draw attention to what follows
The ا at the end means 'we/us'
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the past-tense verb أُوحِىَ (was revealed) that comes after it. It's like saying 'it was definitely revealed' instead of just 'it was revealed'
Added a sukun (ْ) on the د to show where the word stops

steps.m: to reveal or inspire (especially divine knowledge)

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he revealed' in the past tense

steps.m: it was revealed (subject of قَدْ in إِنَّا قَدْ)
steps.n: Changed to passive form ('was revealed' instead of 'revealed') by adding أُ at start and changing middle vowel. Shows someone received the revelation rather than gave it. Links to قَدْ in the verse.
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. This word doesn't come from a root - it's a basic preposition.
Added ي and sukun (ْ) to prepare for connecting with the next part
Added نا at the end to show this means 'us'. This word receives the action of أُوحِىَ (it was revealed) in the ayah.
Added the long alif (آ) at the end for emphasis in recitation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the word that connects parts of sentences, like saying 'I know that he came' in English

steps.m: that (introduces what was revealed in أُوحِىَ)
steps.n: Adding the shadda makes the word stronger and shows it's connecting to something important that follows. Here it introduces what was revealed.
Changed from verb to noun meaning the punishment itself. The word is the object of أَنَّ (that) in the phrase 'that the punishment'.
Added ال to make it specific/definite. The َ (fatha) at the end shows this is the object of أَنَّ (that).
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects to the word مَن (whoever) that comes after it.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show this is a special ending called 'alif maksura'. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with proper reading.
This is a special word used to refer to people in a general way. Here it means 'whoever' or 'anyone who' and is the subject (doer) of the verb كَذَّبَ (denied/rejected).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he denied repeatedly/strongly (the warning of punishment mentioned in أَنَّ ٱلْعَذَابَ)
steps.n: Added shadda to show intense or repeated denial. This connects to the warning of punishment (العذاب) mentioned earlier in the verse that was denied.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled ل to show doing action to oneself
Added و at beginning to connect this action with what came before
Added alif maqsurah at end to show this is from a root ending in ي
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word shows who is asking the question about 'your Lord' (رَّبُّكُمَا) in this ayah.
The middle letter و changed to ا which is common for hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)

steps.m: who (asking about the subject of يٰمُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: This is a question word used to ask about people. Here it's asking Musa about who someone is.

steps.m: so who (asking about the subject of يٰمُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: The فَ shows this question follows from what was said before (قَالَ - he said). It connects the question to the previous statement.
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures completely
The ُ at the end shows this word is the subject or topic
كُمَا means 'your' when talking to two people

steps.m: your (both of you) lord (addressing Moses and Aaron)
steps.n: The shadda (doubled letter) was added to the ر, but the meaning stays the same - 'your Lord' speaking to two people (Moses and Aaron)
This is a proper name
The يٰ is used to call out to someone. In this ayah, Pharaoh is addressing Moses directly after being asked about who their Lord is (رَّبُّكُمَا)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense. This word is what introduces what our Lord (رَبُّنَا) says next.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it has a fatha and comes between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to say.

steps.m: to nurture, care for, raise up
Lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show this is someone who nurtures and cares completely
Added damma to show this is the doer of the action in قَالَ (he said)

steps.m: our Lord (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added نَا to show this is our Lord
This is the base form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things. The long vowel mark (ٓ) helps with pronunciation.
Added ال at the beginning which makes it more specific. The ل combines with ذ to make a stronger 'dh' sound (shown by ّ). This word introduces the description of Allah who 'gave everything its form.'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning to show giving something to someone directly. The و at the end changed to ى because it's easier to say.
Added a small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's a long sound. This word is part of what was said (قَالَ) in the ayah.
Doubling the middle letter makes it stronger - changes from 'complete' to 'all'
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of 'gave' (أَعْطَىٰ) in the phrase 'gave every thing its creation'
Root letters ش-ي-أ become the noun شَيْء meaning 'thing'. The hamza (ء) at the end shows it's a thing being acted on.
The ٍ (kasratan) at the end shows this is 'of a thing' - like saying 'every thing' becomes 'of every thing'

The notes are incorrect - there is no actual change in the tanwin kasra between these words. The only difference is the spelling of hamza (ء) with different letter forms, but the pronunciation and meaning stay the same.

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something

steps.n: Changed from an action to a thing by adding vowel marks. This word refers to the unique form or nature that Allah gave to each thing He created.

steps.m: its creation (what was given to everything)
steps.n: Added هُ to show possession ('its/his') and َ to show this is what was given (object of أَعْطَىٰ). The ۥ is just a pronunciation mark.
This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another, like saying 'first this, then that'. In this ayah, it connects 'gave everything its form' with 'guided'

steps.m: to guide (to show the right way)

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to make it past tense
Changed final ي to ى because it's at the end of the word
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced like a long a sound
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word introduces what someone said, which in this case is the question that follows.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
This is a compound of فَ (so/then) and مَا (what)
Changed from root letters to past tense verb by adding vowel marks (fatha)
Added damma ending to make it a noun in nominative case

steps.m: to join together in time or space
a generation (group of people living in the same time period)
steps.n: Root means 'join together' - becomes a noun meaning 'people joined by living in same time'
Changed the middle vowel to 'u' and added 'uu' at the end to make it plural (like book → books)
Added 'the' (ال) at the start and a kasra (ِ) at the end to show it's connected to the word بَالُ (asking about the state of these generations)

steps.m: to come first or be at the beginning

steps.n: Changed the root letters to make the feminine word meaning 'first' by adding vowel marks (damma ُ and fatha َ)
Added ى at the end to make it into the special feminine form used for superlatives and ordering

steps.m: the first ones (referring to ٱلْقُرُونِ - the previous generations)
steps.n: Added the definite article ٱلْ to specify these particular first/former ones, and added the small alif (ٰ) at the end which is a writing variation of ى
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is connected to عِلْمُهَا (its knowledge) which comes after it in the ayah.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Changed the root into a noun by adding the vowel pattern ِ_ْ, which turns an action into the concept itself
The ُ shows this word is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah

steps.m: its knowledge (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'its' here, referring back to something feminine mentioned before. The word remains the subject of قَالَ (he said)
The kasra changes this into a preposition showing where something is located. In this ayah, it shows where 'its knowledge' (عِلْمُهَا) is located.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used as a preposition
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a title/role, emphasizing complete nurturing authority
The kasra shows this word is connected to كِتٰبٍ (book) in the phrase 'in a book'

steps.m: my Lord (subject of لَّا يَضِلُّ - 'does not err')
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this is 'my Lord'. This Lord is the subject of لَّا يَضِلُّ (does not err) in this ayah
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. Here it connects to the word كِتٰبٍ (book) to show where something is located.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he wrote' into 'something written' by adding an 'i' sound after first letter and 'aa' in middle
The word gets two dots under it (ٍ) because it comes after 'in' (فِى). The straight line over the 'a' (ٰ) is just a different way of writing it.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative, changing 'goes astray' to 'does not go astray'
The shadda is added because this word comes after a word ending in a similar sound, causing them to merge in pronunciation. This is a common rule in Quranic recitation but doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form

steps.m: he loses the way (subject of لَّا in this ayah)
steps.n: Changes to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, adding shadda to show doubled letter, and adding damma at end. This word comes after لَّا (not) in the ayah.
The word gets a damma (ُ) ending to show it's in the normal subject form
Doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and cares completely
The kasra shows this word is connected to something that comes after it
Adding ى at the end shows this is 'my' Lord. This word appears with عِندَ (with) in the ayah.
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it

steps.m: and not (connected to يَضِلُّ - he loses track)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement. This connects 'does not lose track' with 'and does not forget'.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the past tense form
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense. The final ي changed to ى because it's at the end of a present tense verb.
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. The ل of the definite article assimilates with the following letter causing shadda.

steps.m: he made/set (subject of الَّذِى - the One who)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is a past tense verb. In this ayah, this word describes what Allah did, as indicated by الَّذِى (the One who) before it.
Added fatha to show the basic meaning of direction or purpose

steps.m: for you all (object of جَعَلَ - He made)
steps.n: Added كُمُ to show this is being done for multiple people. This word connects to جَعَلَ (He made) showing who something was made for.
Basic noun form of 'earth/land' with standard vowel marks
Added ال to make it specific ('the earth') and fatha at the end shows it's receiving the action of جَعَلَ (made/placed)

steps.m: a cradle, spread-out place (object of جَعَلَ)
steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun describing a smooth, spread-out place. This word is what Allah made (جَعَلَ) the earth to be - like a comfortable cradle.

steps.m: a cradle, spread-out place (as object of جَعَلَ)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this word is receiving the action of جَعَلَ (made/rendered). It's like saying 'Allah made the earth [into] a cradle.'

steps.m: to make a path, to insert something into a path
he made paths (for لَكُمْ - for you)
steps.n: Adding fatha vowels creates the past tense verb. In this ayah, Allah is the subject who made the paths for people.

steps.m: and he made paths (for لَكُمْ - for you)
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous statement about how Allah made the earth as a resting place
Added fatha to the letter ل to show it's a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'
Added كُمْ which means 'you all'. This word is receiving the action of جَعَلَ (he made) in this ayah - Allah made the earth for you all.
This is the basic preposition في which shows location or being inside something

steps.m: in it (feminine, referring to الْأَرْضَ/the earth)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this refers to something feminine (in this case, the earth/الْأَرْضَ mentioned earlier in the ayah)
Added damma vowels to make it plural, showing multiple paths

steps.m: paths/ways (object of سَلَكَ - made for you)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is receiving the action - Allah is making these paths for people to use
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes someone cause the action instead of doing it themselves - like changing 'go down' to 'make go down'
The و connects this to the previous action in the verse
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word 'the sky'. This combination means 'from the sky'
Adds اء to make it a noun meaning sky/heaven
Makes the noun definite
Added kasra at the end and lengthened the middle vowel to show this word follows مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic noun
The middle و changes to ا and final ه changes to hamza (ء) due to word pattern
The long alif (آ) is added to show this is a strong/intense form of water

steps.m: water (object of أَنزَلَ - He sent down)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object being sent down from the sky in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning makes the meaning causative - showing someone making something else do the action

steps.m: we brought out (the plants mentioned later in أَزْوٰجًا مِّن نَّبَاتٍ)
steps.n: This is a past tense verb where 'we' is doing the action. The object being brought out is explained later as 'varieties of plants'

steps.m: and so we brought out (after sending down water)
steps.n: The فَ shows this bringing out happened as a result of sending down water (وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً) mentioned just before
Added kasra to the preposition ب to connect it with the next part
Added ه (hi) at the end to mean 'it' - like saying 'with it' instead of just 'with'
Added pronunciation helpers (small و and elongation mark) that don't change the meaning

steps.m: to join things together in pairs

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to make it into a noun meaning one pair or type
Added أَ at the start and ا after و to make it plural, showing there are many pairs or types
Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of أَخْرَجْنَا (We brought forth)
Basic preposition showing source, origin, or type. In this ayah, it connects with نَّبَاتٍ (plants) to show what something is made from or consists of.
The 'n' sound gets doubled (shown by the shadda mark) because it comes after a pause in speech

steps.m: to grow or sprout from the ground

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it a noun meaning the result of growing
Added shadda on first letter for emphasis
Added tanween kasra because it comes after مِّن (from/of) and describes the kinds/pairs (أَزْوٰجًا) that Allah brought forth
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added shadda to show complete separation and removed final ta
Added ى at the end to make it mean 'different kinds/various' - it describes the plants (نَّبَاتٍ) in the verse
Added dagger alif above the alif maqsura to show correct pronunciation
The initial hamza (أ) is dropped in the command form
Added وا to make it plural - now it's addressing multiple people
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb. The ى replaces ي at the end because it's a weak letter.
Changed to command form by adding اِ at start and putting sukun on middle letter. The weak letter ى drops off in command form.
Added وْا to make it address multiple people (like saying 'you all')

steps.m: and graze/feed! (command to group) (connected to كُلُوا۟)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at start to connect it with the previous command كُلُوا۟ (eat). The همزة changes to ٱ after و.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
The word changed from a verb meaning 'to bless' into a plural noun meaning 'cattle' - which were seen as one of God's blessings
Adding the plural possessive pronoun suffix كم to indicate ownership by multiple people
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. The doubled ن (shown by ّ) makes the emphasis stronger, like saying 'most certainly' instead of just 'certainly'.
Basic preposition showing location or position
Added ى at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects 'in' to the word ذٰلِكَ (that), showing where the signs/proofs are found.
This is the basic pointing word used to refer to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show this points to something far away instead of nearby
Added لِكَ at the end to make the pointing stronger and clearer, like saying 'that one there' instead of just 'that'

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Adding ة changes it from a verb into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'

steps.m: signs (multiple things that show or prove something)
steps.n: Adding ات makes it plural, showing there are multiple signs
Adding ل shows these signs belong to or are for something
The two dots at the end show this is indefinite (not talking about specific signs)
This is a special plural form meaning 'those who possess/have' something. It's used before another noun to show ownership or having a quality.
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction - like saying 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for those who possess (connected to ءَايٰتٍ/signs)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize the meaning. This word connects to ءَايٰتٍ (signs) in the ayah, showing these are signs for people of understanding.
Changed from basic root meaning 'prevent' into noun meaning 'prevention' by adding vowel marks
The word shifts to mean 'intelligence' because it's what prevents people from doing wrong things. The ى at the end makes it plural.
Added ال (the) to make it refer to 'the people of intelligence' - those who can understand right from wrong. The shadda (double letter) appears because ال is added to a word starting with ن.
Added alif maksura at the end to show it's a special plural form
Added hamza to show this is a pause point in recitation

steps.m: from (preposition showing starting point or origin)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it's used to show where Allah will bring people out from.

Adding ها at the end makes it refer to something feminine - in this case, the earth (الأرض) which was mentioned earlier. The full phrase مِنْهَا نُخْرِجُكُمْ means 'from it (the earth) We will bring you out'.

steps.m: to create from nothing, to bring into existence
steps.n: This is one of the most important roots in the Quran, used for divine creation
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it past tense 'he created'
Changed to 'We created' by adding نا at the end, showing Allah speaking as 'We' (royal plural)

steps.m: We created you all (referring to all humans)
steps.n: Added كُمْ (kum) at the end to show 'you all' are the ones being created

steps.m: in (location where نُعِيدُكُمْ/we will return you happens)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing where something happens. In this ayah, it shows where Allah will return us.

steps.m: in it/her (referring to earth/الأرض which was mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Adding ها makes it refer back to the earth (الأرض) which was mentioned before as the place we were created from

steps.m: and in it/her (the earth where نُعِيدُكُمْ/we will return you)
steps.n: Adding و at the start means 'and'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by adding نُ at start
Added كُمْ to show that 'you all' are receiving the action (we return you all)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from

steps.m: from it (referring to الأَرْضِ 'the earth' mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Adding ها makes it refer back to the earth (الأَرْضِ) which was mentioned earlier in the conversation

steps.m: and from it (referring to الأَرْضِ 'the earth')
steps.n: Adding و at the start means 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added 'nu' at start to mean 'we', and changed middle vowel to make it mean 'bring out' instead of 'come out'

steps.m: we bring you all out (from the earth, as mentioned in مِنْهَا)
steps.n: The pronoun كُمْ shows who is being brought out. This connects to مِنْهَا (from it) referring to the earth mentioned earlier.
This root is about something happening repeatedly or in turns
The middle letter و changes to ا to make it easier to say, while keeping the meaning of taking turns
Adding ة makes it refer to one specific instance
The tanween (ً) shows this is the object of the verb 'we bring you out' (نُخْرِجُكُمْ)
This root is about things coming after or being different from what came before
Changed the root letters to make the feminine form meaning 'another/different' by adding ى at the end and putting vowels on the letters
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding hamza at the beginning makes the meaning causative - changing from 'seeing' to 'making someone else see' (showing)
Changed the final ى to ي with sukun to prepare for adding suffixes
Adding نا changes it from 'showed' to 'we showed'
Adding هُ shows who received the action (him)
Forms the basic noun 'sign' from the root. The middle ي becomes ا due to weak letter rules.
Forms regular feminine plural by adding ات suffix. This word is the object (what was shown) in the sentence where Allah is saying 'We showed him Our signs.'
Adding نَا shows that these signs belong to 'us'. This word is the object (what was shown) in the sentence where Allah says 'We showed him our signs.'

steps.m: to make complete, to make whole

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Adding fatha at the end makes it accusative case (used as object)

steps.m: all of them (referring to Our signs)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show this refers to 'Our signs' (ءَايٰتِنَا) mentioned earlier in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning intensive - complete rejection rather than simple lying

steps.m: and so he denied (after أَرَيْنٰهُ ءَايٰتِنَا - We showed him Our signs)
steps.n: Adds فَ meaning 'and so' or 'then' to show this denial came as a result of being shown the signs

steps.m: to refuse or reject something stubbornly

steps.n: Added fatha vowels and the final ى replaces the root letter ي because it's at the end of the word. This word follows فَكَذَّبَ (and he denied) in the ayah, showing a sequence of rejection.
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with the previous one (فَكَذَّبَ - and he denied), showing he both denied AND refused
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) which is a spelling variation of the final ى

steps.m: he said (subject of أَجِئْتَنَا - 'Have you come to us')
steps.n: Added fathas (َ) after each letter to make it past tense. This word is what Pharaoh said before asking Moses a question.
The و (waw) changes to ا (alif) because it's a hollow verb - this is a common change in Arabic verbs
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed vowels and added تَ to show 'you' did the action in the past
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question

steps.m: did you come to us? (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Added نَا meaning 'us/to us'. This word is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha) to make it mean 'he left' in the past
Adds 'made someone' to the action - changes from doing it yourself to making others do it
Changes from 'he' to 'you' doing the action
Adds لِ (li) at start to mean 'so that' or 'in order to'

steps.m: so that you make us leave (from أَرْضِنَا - our land)
steps.n: The word changed by adding 'us' (نَا) at the end to show that Moses would make them (Pharaoh and his people) leave their land
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects to 'our land' (أَرْضِنَا) to show what they want to be driven from
Added marks (fatha and sukun) to make it readable - this is the basic way to say 'land'
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنْ (from)
Added نَا at the end to show it's our land. This word comes after مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'from our land'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'magic' by adding the vowels ِ and ْ
Added ِ to show this magic belongs to something or someone
Added كَ to show the magic belongs to you
Added بِ at the beginning to show this magic is being used as a tool. In this ayah, Pharaoh is accusing Moses (موسى) of trying to use magic against him.
This is the proper name Moses (مُوسَىٰ). The ىٰ at the end is written with a special mark (called alif maqsurah) because it's a name borrowed from Hebrew.
Added يٰ at the beginning to call out to Moses directly. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English. The little mark above يٰ shows it's stretched when calling out.
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Adding ن at the start shows that 'we' are doing the action
Adding لَ at the start means 'surely/definitely' - it makes a strong promise
Adding ف shows this is happening as a result of something else

steps.m: so most surely we will come
steps.n: Adding emphatic نّ suffix to emphasize the certainty of the action

steps.m: so most surely we will come to you
steps.n: The letter ك (kaf) was added at the end to mean 'to you', showing who will be receiving the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he did magic' (verb) to just 'magic' (noun) by changing the vowels
Adds preposition ب meaning 'with/by'

steps.m: with a magic (object of فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ - we will surely bring to you)
steps.n: Adds tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite (a/an) and is affected by the preposition بِ (with). This word is what will be brought in the phrase 'we will surely bring to you'
Changed vowels to create a noun meaning 'similar' or 'like'
Changed the ending to kasra to show it's being used after a preposition

steps.m: like it/similar to it (referring to سِحْر/magic)
steps.n: Added هِۦ to show this is 'similar to it', referring back to the magic (سِحْر) mentioned earlier
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he made' to 'you make!' by adding alif at start - makes it a command to one person
Added فَ prefix to connect this command to the previous statement, showing sequence or result

steps.m: to separate or be distinct from

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a preposition showing space or relationship between things
Added نَا at the end to show this means 'between us'. This word is being acted on by the command اجْعَلْ (make/appoint) in the phrase 'make between us and you an appointment'.
Adding vowels changes this from a root into a common preposition
Adding كَ shows this relates to 'you'. This connects to the appointment (موعدا) mentioned in the ayah.
Adding وَ connects this to بَيْنَنَا (between us) mentioned earlier in the ayah, showing both sides of the agreement

steps.m: to promise or set a time/place to meet

steps.n: Added مَ at start and ِ after ع to create a noun meaning the time or place where something happens
Added ًا at the end to show this is the object (thing receiving the action) of the verb فَٱجْعَلْ (make/set)
The word gets an alif at the end to show it's receiving the action
This is the basic negative word that makes things not happen. In this ayah, it's used to say they will not break the appointment.

steps.m: not at all (negates نُخْلِفُهُ - we break it)
steps.n: Adding the shadda makes the meaning stronger, showing they definitely will not break the appointment.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense 'we' form. This word relates to breaking the appointment (مَوْعِدًا) mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: we will break it (the مَوْعِدًا - appointment)
steps.n: Added هُۥ to show what is being broken - it refers back to the appointment (مَوْعِدًا) mentioned earlier
This is the basic form of the personal pronoun meaning 'we'

steps.m: we (as the subject of نُخْلِفُهُ - we break it)
steps.n: Added dots and marks to show 'we' is doing the action. The mark at the end (ُ) shows we are the ones doing something.
This is the basic negative word that means 'not' or 'no'. The fatha helps us pronounce it correctly.
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but the meaning stays the same
This is a basic personal pronoun meaning 'you' when talking to one male person. It's made up of three letters that work together: أ (hamza), ن (nun), and ت (ta).
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation: a fatha (َ) after أ and another fatha after ت

steps.m: to exist or be in a place

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to show where something happens, and added ا after ك to make it a noun meaning 'place'

steps.m: a place (that we should make neutral between us)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object of اجْعَلْ (make/set) in the phrase 'set between us... a place'
Changes from verb to feminine noun meaning 'equal/level'
Added tanween (double fatha) to show this is describing an indefinite place
Added vowel marks to make the past tense form. This word is connected to مَوْعِدُكُمْ (your appointment) which comes after it in the ayah.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)

steps.m: to promise, appoint, or set a time

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning and ِ after ع to create a noun meaning the time or place when something is promised or appointed
The ُ shows this word is connected to يَوْمُ (day) in the sentence - it means 'the appointment is the day'
كُمْ means 'your' when talking to multiple people
Basic noun form with fatha-sukoon pattern. This word describes what the appointment (موعد) is - it's the day of decoration.
Gets a damma ending because it's part of a phrase meaning 'the day of appointment'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (he decorated) to a noun (decoration) by adding ة at the end and changing the middle vowel
Added ٱل (al-) at the beginning to mean 'the'. This causes the first letter to get a shadda (double mark)
Added kasra at the end because this word belongs to يَوْمُ (day) in the phrase 'day of decoration'
This is a basic particle that introduces a new clause. Here it connects to the verb يُحْشَرَ (gather) to show what will happen.
Added و at the beginning to connect this clause with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to make it passive - showing someone is being gathered rather than gathering others
Changed the ending vowel to fatha to show this is dependent on another verb

steps.m: to be social or familiar with others

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun for people who are social/familiar with each other
Added emphasis by doubling the ن
Added ٱل to specify these particular people
Added ُ to show this is the subject being gathered in يُحْشَرَ (to be gathered)
Added damma and sukun to transform the concept into a noun for morning brightness

steps.m: morning time (when Moses agreed to meet)
steps.n: Changed final و to ى and added tanween to specify a particular time of morning. This word relates to the time Moses set for the meeting mentioned in يَوْمُ ٱلزِّينَةِ (the day of festival).

steps.m: to turn away or take charge of something

steps.n: Added the shadda (double letter mark) to show strong or intense action
Added تَ at start to show doing action to oneself
Added ى at end to complete past tense verb

steps.m: and then he turned himself away
steps.n: Added فَ at start to show this happened after something else

steps.m: and then he turned himself away
steps.n: Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced longer

steps.m: Pharaoh (a title meaning 'great house' in ancient Egyptian)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced. The ِ (kasra) goes under ف, the ْ (sukun) goes above ر, and و is a long vowel.

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the subject who turned away in فَتَوَلَّىٰ)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Pharaoh is doing the action in the sentence - he is the one who 'turned away' (فَتَوَلَّىٰ)

steps.m: to gather or collect things together

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters together. This shows Pharaoh did this action in the past.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after the previous action (فَتَوَلَّىٰ - he turned away)
Changed from action to the thing itself (like changing 'to plot' to 'a plot')
Added fatha at the end to show this is being acted upon by the verb جَمَعَ (gathered)
Added pronoun 'his' to show whose plot it is. This whole word is what Pharaoh gathered, as mentioned in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that connects events in sequence
Added damma (ُ) and shadda with fatha (َّ) to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects Pharaoh gathering his plan (فَجَمَعَ) with him coming (أَتَىٰ)
Added fatha marks to show past tense and make it mean 'he came'

steps.m: he came (after فَجَمَعَ - then he gathered)
steps.n: The final ي changes to ى with an alif above it (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word. This is a common spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning. This word comes after 'then he gathered' (فَجَمَعَ) to show what happened next.
Added fathas to make basic past tense form
Common change where middle weak letter becomes long alif
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of the preposition

steps.m: to/for them (who Musa is speaking to)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show who is being referred to. This word connects to the next part where Musa speaks to these people.
This is the base form of the proper name Moses, borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian into Arabic.
Added emphasis to the first letter with shadda and vowel marks to show proper pronunciation.
Added the alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is standard in Arabic proper names. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
This is a noun meaning complete destruction or severe punishment. It's often used as an expression of warning.
Added fatha to prepare the word for connection with the next part

steps.m: woe to you! (warning to the people Musa is addressing)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being warned. This is addressed to multiple people that Musa is warning about lying about Allah.
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling us not to do the action in تَفْتَرُوا (fabricate/invent falsehood).
Added fatha mark on ل to show how to pronounce it correctly
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding اِفْتَ (ifta) at the start changes the meaning from actual cutting to making up something false
Changes to present tense with 'you' (singular) subject
Changes subject to plural masculine 'you all'
This is a preposition that shows something being on top of or against something else. In this ayah, it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to show what is being lied against.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Changed from the general word for 'a god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال and joining letters together
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of the word عَلَى (upon/against) in the phrase 'do not invent lies against Allah'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and turn the action into a noun.

steps.m: a lie (object of تَفْتَرُوا - to fabricate)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this is both indefinite (like 'a' in English) and is being acted upon by the verb تَفْتَرُوا (to fabricate/invent).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to show future tense
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'and' or 'so'

steps.m: and he will destroy you all completely
steps.n: Added كُم at end to show the action affects 'you all'
Transforms from root concept into noun indicating intense punishment
Added بِ at the beginning to show this punishment is being used as a tool or means
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is referring to an undefined punishment
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. قَدْ is a particle that adds emphasis and shows something is complete or definite.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what comes after it. This connects to the word خَابَ (failed/lost) that follows.

steps.m: to fail or lose out completely

steps.n: Added basic past tense voweling pattern
The middle letter changes to alif which is common for hollow verbs
Basic form of the relative pronoun used to ask about or refer to people
Added kasra at the end to connect it with the following verb ٱفْتَرَىٰ (invented/fabricated). This shows that 'whoever' is doing the action of inventing/fabricating.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: he invented falsely (subject of خَابَ - failed/lost)
steps.n: Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show he did this action to himself. The ى with alif above (ىٰ) at the end shows it's 'he' who did it. This word connects to خَابَ (failed/lost) showing who failed.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added تَ at start and ا after first letter to show people doing something to each other

steps.m: and then they argued with each other
steps.n: Added فَ at start to show this happened after something else
Added وا at end to show many people did this action together
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun form meaning 'matter' or 'affair'
Added هُم at the end to show possession by a group (their)

steps.m: to be separate or clear from one another

steps.n: Adding vowels changes it from a root into a usable word meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them (referring to the magicians who are discussing their plan)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this means 'between them'. In this ayah, 'them' refers to the magicians who are discussing their plan among themselves.
Added shadda to show emphasis and connected letters with fatha
Adding أَ at start makes it about concealing something specific (in this case النَّجْوَىٰ - their private conversation)
Adding وا shows many people did this action together

steps.m: and they concealed (النَّجْوَىٰ - their private conversation)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this action to what happened before
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from verb to noun meaning the secret conversation itself. Added sukun on middle letter and ى at end.
Added ال to make it definite and shadda on ن. The alif at the start has a waṣla mark showing it connects to previous word. Added long alif (ٰ) at end.
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb pattern
Adding وا makes it mean 'they' instead of 'he'

steps.m: indeed (emphasizing what follows in هٰذٰنِ لَسٰحِرٰنِ)
steps.n: This is an emphasis particle that strengthens the meaning of what comes after it. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the speakers are certain about what they're saying about the two magicians.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) above the letters in this Quranic spelling

steps.m: these two (subject of لَسٰحِرٰنِ - indeed magicians)
steps.n: Adding نِ at the end changes it from singular 'this' to dual 'these two'. This word is describing the two magicians mentioned in لَسٰحِرٰنِ (indeed magicians)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changes from verb (to do magic) to noun (person who does magic)
Added the dual ending ان to show exactly two magicians
Added لَ at start to emphasize 'indeed/truly' these are two magicians
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start. The middle letter changed to ي to make it easier to say.

steps.m: they both want (referring to هٰذٰنِ/these two)
steps.n: Added انِ to show two people are doing the action. This verb refers to the two magicians (هٰذٰنِ) mentioned earlier in the ayah.

steps.m: that (connects to يُخْرِجَاكُم - they expel you)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects what the magicians 'want' (يُرِيدَانِ) with what they want 'to do' (يُخْرِجَاكُم - expel you).
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to causative form (making someone do the action) in present tense
Changed to subjunctive mood, used after certain words like أن (that)
Added alif at end to show two people doing the action

steps.m: those two make you all exit
steps.n: Shows who is being made to exit (you all)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. It cannot be broken down into smaller parts.
Added shadda on م and sukun on ن to show correct pronunciation. This word connects to أَرْضِكُم (your land) to show what they want to remove you from.
Connected the root letters together with basic vowel marks to make it pronounceable
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something that comes after it

steps.m: your (all of you) land (that Pharaoh's people are speaking about to each other)
steps.n: Added كُم to show the land belongs to all the people being spoken to. This appears in a conversation where Pharaoh's people are talking about their land that they fear Moses and Aaron want to take from them.
Root letters combined with vowels to make the noun meaning 'magic'
Added هِمَا (their) at the end to show it belongs to two people
Added preposition ب to indicate 'by means of'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added alif at end to show two people doing the action
Added conjunction و to connect with previous statement

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels changes it from basic root to word meaning 'path'
Added ة to specify one particular way or method
Added كُمُ to show the way belongs to multiple people being addressed
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is the means or method by which something is done
Added damma and sukun to make it into a noun meaning something similar
Changed to feminine form and means 'best/most' instead of just 'similar' - like turning 'good' into 'best'
Adding definite article to make the word definite
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens after something else
Changed from 'he gathered' to 'I gather' by adding أ at start and changing vowels to show present tense
Added وا at the end to make it a command for a group
Changed the root letters into a noun that means the actual plot or scheme itself

steps.m: your (plural) plot/scheme (object of فَأَجْمِعُوا - so gather)
steps.n: Adds كُمْ at the end to show this plot belongs to multiple people. This is what they are being told to gather in the command فَأَجْمِعُوا (so gather).
This is the basic form of the word that shows something happens after something else

steps.m: then (after فَأَجْمِعُوا۟ كَيْدَكُمْ/so gather your plan)
steps.n: Added marks to make it mean 'then' - the double م sound shows this happened after the previous action
Changed from basic verb 'come' into a command telling one person to come. The ٱ at start shows it's a command.
Added وا to make it address multiple people instead of just one person

steps.m: to line up or arrange in order

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning the result of lining up

steps.m: in a row/line (describing how they should come)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this describes how they should come (in the context of ٱئْتُوا۟ صَفًّا meaning 'come in a row')
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. قَدْ is a particle that adds emphasis to what comes after it.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This connects it to the previous command فَأَجْمِعُوا۟ (so gather).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone/something succeed'
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun
Adds fatha to show this is the time when they should gather their plot
Adds definite article to specify this particular day
Basic interrogative/relative pronoun meaning 'who' or 'whoever'. In this ayah, it's asking 'who' in relation to the verb اسْتَعْلَىٰ (rose high/prevailed)
Added kasra at the end to connect smoothly with the next word اسْتَعْلَىٰ (rose high/prevailed). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding a-a-a vowels
Adding است prefix changes meaning to seeking/requesting the base verb's action
Adding ى at the end completes the Form X verb pattern
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) to indicate the long vowel sound
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed from قَوَلَ by replacing weak letter و with ا
Added وا at the end to show many people are doing the action. This word shows what the magicians said to Moses.
This is the basic name 'Moses' in Arabic
Added يٰ at the beginning to show we're calling out to Moses, like saying 'Hey Moses!' in English
Added the elongation mark (ٰ) and the small alif (ٓ) at the end to show how it should be pronounced with a long sound at the end when calling out
This is the basic conditional particle meaning 'if'
Added ما changes the meaning from 'if' to 'only/just' - makes it restrictive rather than conditional
The ن and م combine to make a stronger م sound (with shadda), changing the word from 'if what' to 'either/whether'
The final alif is lengthened to add emphasis

This is a connecting word that joins two parts of the sentence. Here it connects to the verb تُلْقِىَ (you throw), showing what the magicians are talking about - whether to throw first or let Moses throw first.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense addressing Moses. The magicians are giving him a choice about who should throw first.
This is the basic conditional particle that gives a choice between options. The آ at the end is just a spelling variation of ا.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this option to something else. In this ayah, it's connecting to what مُوسَىٰٓ (Moses) said before.
This is a particle that introduces what the magicians are saying to Moses - they are giving him two choices about who will throw first
Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past tense verb
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' as the subject, and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: that we be (after أَن in أَن نَّكُونَ)
steps.n: The word changes to show it comes after 'that' (أَن) - the first letter gets doubled (shadda) and the ending changes to show this connection
Connected the root letters أ-و-ل with short vowels (fat-ha) to make the basic word for 'first'

steps.m: first (in the sequence of مَنْ أَلْقَىٰ - who threw)
steps.n: Adding shadda emphasizes that this is definitely the first one in the sequence. In this ayah, it's describing who will be first to throw in the competition with Musa (Moses).

steps.m: who (asking about the person who will throw first)
steps.n: This is a question word asking about which person will do something. In this ayah, it's asking who will be the first to throw.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Adding أَ at the beginning makes this a Form IV verb, which often means 'to make something happen' or 'to do something to something else'
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is the end of the word. It doesn't change the meaning.
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
Changed from قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
This is a particle that changes or corrects what was said before. Here it shows that instead of just saying (قَالَ), something else happened.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make someone do the action'

steps.m: all of you throw! (command to a group)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show this is a command to many people, not just one person. The small circle (۟) shows this is the end of the word.
This is a particle that indicates something happening suddenly or at a specific time

steps.m: and then/and suddenly (introduces what happened after أَلْقُوا - they threw)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows what happened before. In this ayah, it shows what happened after they threw their ropes and sticks.
Changes from singular 'rope' to plural 'ropes' by adding ِ at start and ا in middle
Added ُ to show these ropes are doing the action in يُخَيَّلُ (appears/seems)
Added هُمْ to show these ropes belong to them (the magicians)

steps.m: stick/staff (something used for striking or support)

steps.n: Changes from singular 'staff' to plural 'sticks/staffs'
Added conjunction وَ (and) at the beginning of the word
Added damma (ُ) to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added possessive pronoun suffix هم meaning 'their'
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'causing someone else to imagine'
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and ُ at end, making it mean 'it seems' instead of 'made seem'
Basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something
Added sukun before attaching pronoun
Added pronoun 'him' to show who is being moved towards. This connects to the phrase 'from their magic' that follows it.

steps.m: from (connects سِحْرِهِمْ to what came before)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing that something comes from or originates from something else. Here it connects 'their magic' (سِحْرِهِمْ) to what was mentioned before.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding the kasra (ِ) and sukun (ْ) pattern
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something

steps.m: their magic (referring to the magicians' tricks that made their ropes appear to move)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show this belongs to them (the magicians)
Basic particle that introduces a new clause or statement
Added هَا at the end to show this refers to something feminine (in this case, the magicians' ropes and staffs appearing to move)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Adding ت changes it from 'he' to 'she'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added hamza at beginning to make the feeling more active/intentional. Moses (مُوسَىٰ) is doing this action.
Adds sequential conjunction meaning 'then/so'
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. Here it connects 'his self' (نَفْسِهِۦ) to the verb 'he felt' (أَوْجَسَ), showing where the feeling was.
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under ف to show how to pronounce it, and changed the final ي to ى which is a common writing style in the Quran
Root letters were connected with vowels (َ and ْ) to make the noun meaning 'soul/self'
Added kasra at the end because it follows the preposition فِى (in)
Added هِۦ at the end to show it means 'his self'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun that shows one specific instance of feeling fear

steps.m: a state of fear (object of أَوْجَسَ/felt)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is what Musa felt, connecting it to the verb أَوْجَسَ (felt) in the ayah
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water', not derived from an Arabic root
Added damma (ُ) on first letter for pronunciation
Added shadda (ّ) on first letter because it comes after the word فَأَوْجَسَ which ends with a connecting sound
Added fatha (َ) before the alif maqsurah and small alif (ٰ) above it to show proper pronunciation
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb. This is a common change in Arabic for words with و in the middle.
Adding نَا at the end changes 'said' to 'we said'. This word starts a new statement, telling us what Allah and His angels said.
This is one of the basic words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root. It's used to say 'no' or make things negative.
Added fatha to show how to pronounce it. In this ayah, it's telling someone not to do something - specifically not to fear.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and ُ at end to show it's happening now
Changed to a command form by adding ْ at the end. The و drops out because it's a weak letter. This word appears after لَا which makes it mean 'don't fear!'
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'definitely' or 'for sure' in English
Added ك at the end to show this is talking about 'you' (one male person). This word connects to أَنتَ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (you are the highest) in this ayah, emphasizing that 'you' are indeed the highest.
This is a standalone personal pronoun (you) that isn't built from a root
Added vowel marks to show this is being used as the subject (doer). In this ayah, it's saying 'you are the highest'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Changes from 'became high' to 'higher' by adding أ at the start - this makes it a comparative form (like 'higher' vs 'high' in English)
Adding ال (the) makes it definite and changes the meaning from 'higher' to 'the highest/most high'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make something happen'
Added وَ (and) to connect this command with what came before it

steps.m: that which (object of تَلْقَفْ - it will swallow)
steps.n: This is a special word that in this context is being used as a relative pronoun meaning 'that which'. It's connecting the verb تَلْقَفْ (it will swallow) to what follows it (what they made).
This shows something is inside or within something else
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word في (in)
Added ك at the end to show it's your right hand
Added vowels to connect root letters into basic past tense form
Adding sukun at end makes it jussive mood

steps.m: what/that which (refers to what is in يَمِينِكَ)
steps.n: This is a special word that connects parts of a sentence. Here it's used to point to something specific - what is in your right hand. It's like saying 'throw what is in your right hand' in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense singular form
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action
Added pronunciation marks (ٓ and ۟) to help with recitation. This word is the subject of كَيْدُ (plot/scheme) in this ayah.
Adding shadda makes this an emphatic particle that confirms what comes after it
