Adding مَا restricts the meaning to show that something is exclusive or limited

steps.m: to make or create something with skill

steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to plural by adding وا, showing many people did this action
Forms the noun showing the actual trick or plot. In this ayah, it specifically refers to the magicians' deceptive tricks.
Added damma to show this is the subject of what follows in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show someone who does magic as their job or regular activity
Added the tanween kasra ending to show this is any magician (not a specific one) and that it belongs to the word كَيْدُ (plot/scheme) before it
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Added fatha on lam to help with pronunciation. This word negates the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form
Changes past tense to present tense. This word is negated by لا (not) in the ayah, so it means 'does not succeed'
Changes from verb (to do magic) to noun (person who does magic)
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific magician rather than any magician
Adding damma case marker to indicate nominative case
This is a location word that shows where something happens. The vowel marks help pronounce it correctly.
The ُ at the end shows this word is complete by itself and connects to the verb أَتَىٰ (came) in this ayah, telling us where something came.
Added fathas to show this happened in the past
The final ي changes to ى with an alif above it (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word. This is a common spelling change that happens with words ending in ي.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Adding أ at the start makes the verb causative, meaning to make something happen.
Changed to passive voice by changing vowels. This shows the action was done to someone/something (the magicians were made to fall into prostration).

steps.m: and so they were thrown down (subject: السَّحَرَةُ - the magicians)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action happened as a result of what came before. The magicians were thrown down into prostration.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb

steps.m: he did a lot of magic
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: one who does lots of magic
steps.n: Adding alif after the shadda makes it mean someone who does this action as their job or habit
Adding ة turns the word from someone who does magic into a professional title (like saying magician as a job)

steps.m: the magicians (subject of أُلْقِىَ - were thrown)
steps.n: The ال means 'the' specific magicians, and the ending ة with ُ shows it's plural magicians who did the action

steps.m: to prostrate, bow down in worship

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed vowels and doubled middle letter to form plural noun showing group in state of prostration
Added tanween alif to show this describes how the magicians fell down (their state/manner)
The root letters ق-و-ل become قَالَ (he said) by changing و to ا and adding past tense vowels - a common pattern for 'saying' words
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Added elongation mark (ٓ) and small high rounded zero (۟) which are pronunciation helpers that don't change the meaning
Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively believe/trust in something'
The ن (noon) at the end was removed to prepare for adding نا (we) as the subject. This changes it from 'believe' to the start of 'we believed'

steps.m: we believed (said by the magicians to Pharaoh)
steps.n: The word changed from 'believe' to 'we believed' by adding نا (na) at the end, which means 'we'. The shadda shows emphasis in how strongly they believed.

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: Adding the vowel and doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who takes complete care of others
The بِ at the beginning shows this is how or by what means something happens. This word connects to 'Harun and Musa' in the ayah to show whose lord is meant.
Adding the kasra at the end shows this connects to the next words (Harun and Musa)
This is the base form of the proper name for Prophet Aaron, brother of Moses
The alif is written as a small line above the ه to show the long 'aa' sound
Added damma (ُ) on ر to show proper pronunciation
Added fatha (َ) at the end to show this name is connected to موسى (Moses) in the sentence
This is a proper name that doesn't come from a root
Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it, showing that Moses is mentioned along with Aaron (هٰرُونَ) who was mentioned in the previous part

steps.m: he said (introducing what Pharaoh says next)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word introduces what Pharaoh says to his magicians after they believed in Moses.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle

steps.m: to trust or feel safe with something

steps.n: Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively put trust in something'
Added ت at the end to show 'you all' (the magicians) did the believing in the past. Pharaoh is angrily questioning his magicians about their new faith.
Added ُمْ (tum) at the end to change from 'you' to 'you all', showing Pharaoh speaking to multiple magicians who believed
Starting with the basic preposition ل which shows direction or purpose
Added the pronoun ه with damma to show it means 'him'
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to ءَامَنتُمْ (you believed) to show who was believed in.
Changes from verb root to noun meaning 'before'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing when something happened in relation to the action of giving permission
This is a particle that introduces a new clause, similar to 'that' in English
Changed to Form IV verb (not Form III) by adding hamza and alif at start. Shows someone making/letting something happen - here Pharaoh giving permission.
Added fatha to the letter lam to help with pronunciation
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people being addressed
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphatic 'indeed'
Added pronoun هُ (he/it) to show who is being emphasized. This word connects to لَكَبِيرُكُمُ (surely your leader) in the ayah.
Changes root into a noun meaning someone who has this quality (like changing 'lead' to 'leader')
Adds emphatic lam prefix to indicate certainty/emphasis

steps.m: surely (he is) your leader (addressing Pharaoh's magicians)
steps.n: Added possessive pronoun suffix كُمُ meaning 'your (plural)'. In this ayah, Pharaoh is accusing Moses of being the master/leader who taught magic to his magicians.
Added ِى to make it into a relative pronoun that connects descriptions to things or people

steps.m: the one who/which (refers to the one who taught you magic)
steps.n: Added ٱلَّ at the beginning to make it specific and emphatic. In this ayah, it's referring back to the teacher of magic mentioned in the conversation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter adds emphasis to show intensive teaching
Added كُمُ to show who was being taught - in this case, multiple people (you all)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Noun form showing the act or result of magic
Adding ال makes it specific magic, and doubles the س letter

steps.m: the magic (object of عَلَّمَكُمُ - taught you)
steps.n: The fatha shows this is what was taught - it's the object of the verb عَلَّمَكُمُ (taught you)
Doubling the middle letter shows this will be done with force and violence
Added لَ and أُ to show this is a strong first-person future threat

steps.m: I will most certainly cut off
steps.n: The نَّ ending makes this an unbreakable oath or threat

steps.m: so I will most certainly cut off
steps.n: The فَ shows this threat is a direct consequence of their disobedience mentioned before
Root letters form the word for 'hand', which is used for reaching/extending
Changes to broken plural form by adding alif at start and sukun on middle letter
Adds ي to make it a feminine plural noun meaning multiple hands
Adds كُمْ to show possession by multiple people being addressed
Added vowel marks to form the basic singular noun
Changes the singular 'leg' into plural 'legs' by adding أ at start and changing vowels
Added conjunction وَ (and). This word is one of the objects that Pharaoh threatens to cut off, connected to the verb فَلَأُقَطِّعَنَّ (I will surely cut off)
Added possessive pronoun suffix كُم (your) with accusative case ending َ because it's the object of the verb فَلَأُقَطِّعَنَّ (I will surely cut off)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added sukun to show the word ends with a silent ن
Added shadda to show this connects smoothly with the previous word in speech. This مِّنْ connects to خِلٰفٍ to show 'from opposite sides'

steps.m: to be opposite or different from something

steps.n: The ا makes it a noun meaning 'the act of opposing'
Added tanwin kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنْ (from). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.
Added shadda to show intensive meaning of crucifixion
Changes to first person subjunctive mood

steps.m: I will surely lift and fix firmly
steps.n: Adds نَّ at the end to make the meaning stronger - like saying 'I will DEFINITELY crucify' instead of just 'I will crucify'

steps.m: I will surely lift and fix firmly you all
steps.n: Adds 'you all' (كُمْ) to the end to show who will be crucified

steps.m: And surely I will crucify you (pl)
steps.n: Added 'and' (و) at start + added 'surely' (لَ) for emphasis
Basic preposition showing location or position inside something. In this ayah, it connects to جُذُوعِ (trunks) to show where the crucifixion will happen.
Changed from singular 'trunk' to plural 'trunks' by adding u-vowels (damma) and waw
Added kasra at the end because it follows في (on/in) in the phrase 'وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ فِى جُذُوعِ ٱلنَّخْلِ' (and I will surely crucify you on the trunks of palm trees)
Basic noun pattern with fatha and sukun makes it a simple noun

steps.m: the date palms (possessed by جُذُوع - trunks)
steps.n: The word gets 'the' in front (ال) and a stronger 'n' sound (ّ), making it 'the date palms'

steps.m: of the date palms (possessed by جُذُوع - trunks)
steps.n: Adding kasra at end marks genitive case
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added نَّ at the end to add strong emphasis and certainty to the action
Added وَ at start to connect this with the previous statement
Added لَ after وَ to add even more emphasis to the certainty
Added damma to show this is the subject doing the action

steps.m: which of us (subject of أَشَدُّ - more severe)
steps.n: Added نَآ at the end to show this means 'which of us'. This word is asking which person has more severe punishment in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (he became strong) to a noun (strong) by combining the double د with a shadda
Added أَ at beginning and ُ at end to make it comparative, showing it's stronger than the punishment (عَذَابًا) mentioned later in the ayah
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this verb into a noun meaning the result of punishment
The ًا ending shows this is the thing being compared when Pharaoh says 'which of us is more severe in punishment'
Added أ at the beginning and changed the vowels to make it mean 'more' of something
Added 'and' at the beginning to connect it with the previous word. This word is being compared to عَذَابًا (punishment) in the ayah.
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا, which is common in words with و in the middle.
Added وا at the end to show many people (the magicians) are speaking to Pharaoh
This is a negative particle that makes the following verb نُّؤْثِرَكَ (we prefer you) impossible in the future. It's one of the basic particles in Arabic that can't be broken down further.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'we' form and added emphasis with shadda on ن, showing strong preference/choice
Adds كَ to show that 'you' is the object being preferred. This is connected to لَن (will not) earlier in the ayah which negates the verb.
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or over something else. Since it's a particle, it doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: upon/over (something that will be mentioned after)
steps.n: The small alif (ىٰ) at the end shows this word connects to what comes after it. In this ayah, it connects to 'مَا جَآءَنَا' (what came to us).
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'that which' in this context. It connects to the verb نُّؤْثِرَكَ (we will prefer you) and refers to the clear signs (الْبَيِّنٰتِ) mentioned right after it.
The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). Added fathas to show past tense.
Added نَا at the end to show that 'we' are the ones being come to (the object of the verb)
This is a basic preposition showing something comes from somewhere or showing a part of something
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلْبَيِّنٰتِ (the clear proofs). This helps make the words flow together smoothly when reading.
Changed from a verb (to make clear) into an adjective (clear) by adding a shadda
Added feminine ending to make it a noun meaning 'a clear proof'
Changed to feminine plural form
Added kasra to show this comes after مِنَ (from)
Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific/definite
This is the base demonstrative word used to refer to someone or something
Added ال with a shadda on the ل to make it specific

steps.m: and the one who (connected to قَالُوا۟)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement قَالُوا۟ (they said)
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. In this ayah, this refers to Allah's act of creation.

steps.m: created us (object of الَّذِي - The One Who)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show we are the ones being created. This word describes what Allah did, coming after الَّذِي (The One Who).
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Changed from 'he decided' to 'you decide!' by adding alif at start and making it a command
Added فَ at beginning to connect this command with what came before, showing it's a result or consequence
This is a relative pronoun connecting to جَآءَنَا (came to us) in this ayah. The extended alif (آ) is a spelling variation of regular alif (ا).
This is a basic pronoun used to address one male person directly. In this ayah, it's the subject of قَاضٍ (one who decrees/judges), showing who is doing the judging.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action

steps.m: a judge (subject of فَٱقْضِ - so judge/decree)
steps.n: The ي at the end is dropped and replaced with tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite. This word is connected to the command فَٱقْضِ (so judge/decree) in the ayah.
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
When we add مَا to إِنَّ, it changes the meaning from just emphasis to meaning 'only'. This is a special combination in Arabic where two words join to make a new meaning.
Added fathas to connect the letters. The final ي changes to ى (alif maqsurah) because it's at the end of the word.
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on middle letter, and kasra before final letter
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the beginning

Changed final ي to ى (alif maqsurah) since it's at the end of the word. This word is being said to Pharaoh by the magicians who believed in Moses, telling him to make whatever judgment he wants against them.
Basic demonstrative word used to point to things
Added ذا to specify pointing to something near
Changed alif to alif with overbar for pronunciation

steps.m: this (when pointing to something feminine near)
steps.n: Added هِ with kasra to make it feminine, since it points to الْحَيَوٰةَ (life) which is feminine
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he lived' (verb) to 'life' (noun) by adding و and ة at the end
Added ال to make it specific and َ to show it's the object of the verb تَقْضِى (you judge/decide)
Root د-ن-و means 'near' - added vowels and ي to make the word 'worldly' or 'this world'
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the د letter (shadda) because it comes after ال. Extended the final alif for emphasis.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what follows more certain

steps.m: indeed we/truly we (subject of ءَامَنَّا - we believed)
steps.n: The long alif at the end shows this is about 'we'. This word connects to ءَامَنَّا (we believed) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively put trust in something'
Added shadda to emphasize the certainty of belief
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the believing. This word is the subject of إِنَّ (indeed/truly) that appears at the start of the verse.

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does the nurturing completely and perfectly

steps.m: Lord (being described or connected to something)
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) shows this word is being connected to something else
Added نا which means 'our' to show the Lord belongs to us or is connected to us

steps.m: by our Lord (we swear by in إِنَّآ ءَامَنَّا)
steps.n: The بِ shows we are swearing by our Lord in our statement of belief. This connects to the previous words 'Indeed, we believe'

steps.m: to forgive, to pardon, to cover up faults

steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show 'he' is doing the action in the present, and added َ at the end to show this is connected to another word that comes before it

steps.m: so that he may forgive (purpose of ءَامَنَّا - we believed)
steps.n: Added لِ at start to show this explains the purpose or reason for their belief. This connects back to when they said 'we believed' (ءَامَنَّا)
This is the basic preposition that shows something is being done for or to someone/something
Added نَا which means 'us' to show who the action is being done for. This word is connected to يَغْفِرَ (forgive) in the ayah, showing what is being forgiven.

steps.m: to make a mistake or sin

steps.n: Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb form
Changes from single mistake to plural mistakes by adding alif and ya
Adding نا at the end to show possession and changing alifs to long alifs (ٰ)
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement
Changed the final ا to آ, which is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to force, make someone do something unwillingly

steps.n: Adding أ at the beginning makes this Form IV, which adds the meaning of causing someone else to do something
Added نا at the end to show who received the action (us)
This is one of the basic prepositions in Arabic that shows something is on top of or over something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching the pronoun. This is just a spelling change, not a meaning change.

steps.m: upon him (referring to what they were forced to do)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end which means 'him'. This shows who the action is happening to.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word السِّحْرِ (magic). Together they mean 'from the magic'
Changed the verb into a noun that means the practice or art of magic itself
Added ال to make it specific magic, and the س gets a shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah
This is a unique word that is the proper name of God in Arabic. It's believed to come from combining ال (the) with إله (deity), but has become its own distinct word.
Added shadda on ل to show it is pronounced strongly
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement
Changed the alif to a wasla form since it comes after a connecting و
This makes it a noun meaning 'good/better/best'
Added the -un ending (two dots on top) to show this word is the subject - it's telling us that Allah is better/best
Added أ at beginning to make comparative form (more/most). The ى at the end is because this is a weak letter word.
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'. The ٰٓ marks are pronunciation helpers.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain

steps.m: indeed he (refers back to مَن/whoever in the ayah)
steps.n: The هُۥ at the end refers back to مَن (whoever) mentioned in the ayah. It's used to connect this word to who we're talking about.
This is a word that introduces a condition - it means 'whoever' or 'anyone who' does something. In this ayah, it refers to whoever comes (يَأْتِ) to their Lord as a criminal.
Added يَ at the beginning to show 'he' is doing the action in the present/future. The dots under the last letter (ي) show it's happening now.

steps.m: he comes (after إِنَّهُۥ مَن - indeed whoever)
steps.n: The ي at the end is removed because this word comes after مَن (whoever), which makes it conditional - like saying 'whoever comes' instead of just 'he comes'
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and cares completely and perfectly
Adding هُ with the mark ۥ shows this belongs to him (his). This word is receiving the action of يَأْتِ (comes to) - when someone comes to their Lord.

steps.m: to do wrong or commit crimes

steps.n: Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who does the action, turning 'commit crimes' into 'one who commits crimes'

steps.m: a criminal (object of يَأْتِ - comes to)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'coming' in the phrase 'whoever comes as a criminal'
Basic connecting particle that shows what comes next is a result of what came before
Adds emphasis to show what comes next is definitely true. In this ayah, it's emphasizing that Hell is certain for wrongdoers.
This shows something belongs to or is for someone
The هُۥ means 'him'. This word connects to جَهَنَّمَ (Hell) in the ayah, showing Hell is for him.
This is a proper noun for Hell that doesn't come from a root. The shadda on ن shows emphasis.

steps.m: Hell (as the object of يَأْتِ - comes to)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is the object being acted upon in the sentence - the place that sinners will come to.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَمُوتُ (he dies) into 'he does not die'. The fatha helps show it's being used to negate a present/future action.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Changed the first vowel from fatha to damma to show something being affected by death rather than causing death
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense and ُ at the end to show the subject is 'he'. This verb describes what happens to the wrongdoer in Hell.
This is a basic preposition showing something is inside or within something else
Added هَا which means 'it' when talking about something feminine. Here it refers back to Hell (جَهَنَّمَ) which is treated as feminine in Arabic.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
Added لَا which means 'not'. This negates the verb يَحْيَىٰ (lives) that comes after it in the ayah, showing that someone 'does not live'.

steps.m: to live, be alive, or survive

steps.n: Root letters connected with vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he lived'
Added يَ at the start to make it present tense (like adding 'is' in English). The spelling changed at the end because two ي letters can't be next to each other in Arabic.

steps.m: he lives/is alive (in contrast to لَا يَمُوتُ - does not die)
steps.n: Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى which is just a spelling convention. This word contrasts with 'does not die' in the ayah to emphasize eternal life.
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at start to show present tense and added vowel marks. This is the main verb for مَن (whoever) in the ayah.

steps.m: he comes to him (predicate of مَن)
steps.n: Added هِۦ at the end to show who is being come to. The ۦ is a pronunciation mark for the هِ.

steps.m: to believe in or have faith
steps.n: Added an alif at the start to make it mean 'to actively believe/trust in something' instead of just feeling secure
Changed from describing the action to describing the person who does it

steps.m: a believer (as someone who comes to Allah in the ayah)
steps.n: Added ًا (an) at the end to show this is describing someone specific - the person who comes to Allah in this situation
This is a particle that emphasizes the verb عَمِلَ (did/performed) that comes right after it. It's like saying 'has definitely done' or 'has indeed done' in English.

steps.m: he did/worked (subject of يَأْتِهِ - comes to Him)
steps.n: Added vowels to make past tense verb. This shows who did the action (he) and when it happened (in the past). In this ayah, this describes what the person who 'comes to Him' has done.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb

steps.m: one who is good/righteous (doer of صَلَحَ)
steps.n: Changed from a verb (doing good) to describe a person who does good deeds
Added feminine plural ending to refer to multiple good deeds. This word is what is being acted upon by the verb عَمِلَ (did/performed)
Added 'the' (ال) at beginning and changed ending to show it's receiving the action
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to people or things that are far away (like saying 'those people' in English). Here it points back to the believers mentioned before who did good deeds.

steps.m: so those (connecting to what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what was mentioned before - that they were believers who did good deeds.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam
Added ه with damma to show 'him'

steps.m: for them (referring to those who come as believers having done righteous deeds)
steps.n: Added م with damma to change 'him' to 'them', showing this refers to multiple people. This word connects to أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ (those) from the previous part of the ayah.

steps.m: he went up step by step
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added ة to show one single level or step
Added ات to make it plural, showing multiple levels
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the د with shadda because it comes after ال
Added ُ at the end to show this word is the subject, and changed the alif to a long alif (ٰ) for writing style
Changed the root into a noun meaning the quality itself
Changed to feminine form showing the highest degree of this quality
Added the definite article to specify these are the particular highest ranks mentioned in the ayah about paradise

steps.m: to cover or hide (like a garden being hidden by trees)

steps.n: Adding vowels makes it a proper past tense verb
Changed the root word 'to cover/hide' into 'gardens' by doubling the middle letter and adding ات to make it plural
Added ُ to show these gardens are the subject doing the action in تَجْرِى (flows)
This is a proper noun referring to a specific type of garden
The tanween (ٍ) shows this word is describing the gardens (جَنَّاتُ) mentioned before it. Together they mean 'Gardens of Paradise'

steps.m: it flows (referring to الْأَنْهٰرُ - the rivers)
steps.n: Added ت at beginning to show present tense for feminine subject (rivers)

steps.m: it flows (referring to الْأَنْهٰرُ - the rivers)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation with no meaning change

steps.m: from (showing that the rivers flow from beneath the gardens)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from or comes out of. In this ayah, it shows that the rivers flow starting from beneath the gardens of paradise.
Connected the letters with vowel marks. This word shows where الْأَنْهٰرُ (the rivers) are located in relation to جَنَّاتُ (gardens).
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something
Added ها which refers back to جَنَّاتُ (gardens) mentioned earlier
Changed the root meaning 'to flow' into a noun meaning 'river' by adding vowels
Changed to plural form by adding أ at start and ٰ after second root letter
Added ٱلْ to specify these particular rivers (the ones flowing under the gardens mentioned in the ayah)

steps.m: the rivers (which flow under the gardens)
steps.n: Added ُ to show these rivers are the subject of تَجْرِى (flows) in the ayah
Changes from verb 'to remain' to 'one who remains'

steps.m: ones who are staying forever (describes the people in جَنَّاتُ)
steps.n: The ين ending changes it from 'one person staying forever' to 'many people staying forever', showing they will live in the gardens eternally
Basic preposition showing location or containment
Added هَا which refers back to جَنَّاتُ (gardens) mentioned earlier in the ayah. The feminine form is used because جَنَّاتُ is feminine.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes 'this' to 'that', making it point to something farther away
The ك at the end is added to complete the demonstrative pronoun form
The و connects this word to what came before it in the ayah, joining the ideas together

steps.m: to give back what someone deserves
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Changed from basic root meaning 'reward/payback' to a noun meaning 'giving back' by adding vowels and changing ي to ا at the end
Added اء to make it a noun specifically meaning a reward or payback

steps.m: reward (as the main subject of the sentence)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the main subject doing something in the sentence
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it means 'whoever' or 'the one who' and is the subject of the verb تَزَكَّىٰ (purifies himself)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning of purification
Adding ت prefix makes the verb reflexive - doing action to oneself
The alif at the end (ىٰ) is just a writing style for the long ى. This word is the subject of جَزَآءُ (reward) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. قَدْ is a particle that adds emphasis and certainty to what comes after it.
Added لَ at the beginning which adds extra emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added alif at beginning to show causative meaning. The final ى replaces يَ due to weak letter rules.
Changed final ى back to يْ to prepare for suffix addition
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' as the subject, with آ showing emphasis. This verb connects to مُوسَىٰٓ (Moses) who receives the revelation.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to the word مُوسَىٰٓ (Moses) to show the revelation was directed to him.
Added an alif khanjariyah (ىٰ) at the end, which is a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning
This is the base form of the proper name Moses, borrowed from Hebrew/Ancient Egyptian into Arabic. It's not derived from an Arabic root.
Added the basic vowel marks to show pronunciation
Added the alif maqsurah (ى) at the end which is common in Arabic proper names
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced like a long 'aa' sound
Added the small vertical line (ٓ) to show a slight lengthening of the final vowel when pausing at this word
This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of sentences

steps.m: that (connects to أَسْرِ - travel by night)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce it. This particle connects to the verb أَسْرِ (travel by night) that comes after it, telling us what Allah revealed to Moses.
Added fathas to make past tense. The final ي changes to ى when it comes at the end.

steps.m: travel at night! (command to Prophet Moses)
steps.n: Changed to command form by adding أَ at start, putting sukun on س, and kasra on ر. The final ى is dropped in command form.
Root letters put together with vowels to make a word meaning 'one who serves/worships'
Forms broken plural indicating multiple servants
Added preposition ب with kasra to show 'with'

steps.m: with my servants (object of أَسْرِ - travel by night)
steps.n: Added ى with kasra to show possession. This word is the object of the command أَسْرِ (travel by night) given to Moses.
Added sukun on first letter and kasra on middle letter to make it a command form
Added فَ (so/then) at beginning to connect it with previous command, and added ٱ (helper letter) before the command
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of the preposition ل
Added هُ (him) to show who the preposition is directed to
Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them', making it refer to multiple people

steps.m: to make a path by treading

steps.n: Adding ي changes it from action (making a path) to the path itself
Added ًا at the end to show this is the object (the thing receiving the action) - it's the path that Moses will make
This is a basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it shows Moses was commanded to strike a path in the sea.
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a noun meaning a large body of water that splits through land
Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it comes after the word فِى (through)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form
Changed from a verb (dried out) to a describing word (dry) by adding tanween. This tells us the path through the sea was completely dry.
This is the basic negative particle used to negate verbs and statements
The shadda shows this word combines with the previous word أَنْ (that), where the ن sound merges into the ل. This is like saying أَنْ + لا = أَلَّا (that not)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' and changed the middle و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). The ُ at the end shows it's happening now.

steps.m: to catch up with or reach something

steps.n: Adding the basic vowel marks creates the past tense verb form

steps.m: being caught up with (object of لَّا تَخٰفُ - you do not fear)
steps.n: Added ًا (tanween) to change the verb 'caught up' into a noun meaning 'being caught up with' - this is what Musa won't fear from Pharaoh's army
This is the basic negation word that means 'not' or 'no'

steps.m: and not (connected to تَخٰفُ - you fear)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous statement. In this ayah, it connects to the verb تَخٰفُ (you fear), making the complete meaning 'and you do not fear'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense. The final ي changed to ى because it's at the end of a present tense verb.
Added the small alif (ٰ) after ى which is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes it mean 'to make someone follow' instead of just 'to follow'. In this ayah, Pharaoh (فرعون) is making his army follow.
Adding فَ shows this happened right after something else mentioned before

steps.m: and then he made them follow (فرعون made his army follow)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows who was being pursued - 'them' refers to the Children of Israel who were being chased
From ancient Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house', adapted into Arabic with a recognizable nominal pattern
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly. The ِ (kasra) under ف and ْ (sukun) on ر help make the 'fir' sound, while the و with ْ (sukun) makes the 'awn' sound.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Here, Pharaoh is the one who followed (أَتْبَعَ) the Children of Israel.
Forms a noun meaning a single group or collection of soldiers
Changes from singular to plural by changing middle vowel to 'u' sound (like jund to junood)
Adds preposition بِ meaning 'with' to show Pharaoh used his armies as a tool
Adds kasra to connect to the possessive pronoun that follows
Adds possessive pronoun 'his' referring to Pharaoh
Added vowels and the weak letter و changed to ي. This is the past tense form.
Added فَ to show this happened after the previous action (Pharaoh pursuing them)

steps.m: and then it covered/overwhelmed them (referring to the sea covering Pharaoh's army)
steps.n: Added هُم to show who was covered by the sea
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added kasra under م to show how to pronounce it
The م gets doubled (shadda) because it comes right after a word ending in ن or م - this is a common pronunciation rule
Added fatha at the end to connect it with the next word ٱلْيَمِّ (the sea)

steps.m: to go towards, head in a direction

steps.n: Adding shadda doubles the last م to add emphasis
The word 'sea' got 'al' (the) added at the start and a kasra sound at the end because it comes after the word 'from' (مِنَ)
Basic form of the particle without any vowel marks
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with vowels. The weak letter و changes to ي because of the kasra before it. This verb follows فَ (then/so) to show what happened after Pharaoh followed them.

steps.m: he covered/overwhelmed them (referring to Pharaoh's forces being overwhelmed by the sea)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who was overwhelmed (them). This refers to Pharaoh and his army being overwhelmed by the sea.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes it mean 'to make someone else do the action'

steps.m: and he made (someone) lose the way (subject: فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it. In this ayah, Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنُ) is doing the action.
Adding أَ changes meaning from 'going astray' to 'making others go astray'
From ancient Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house', adapted into Arabic with a recognizable nominal pattern
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced in Arabic. The kasra (ِ) under ف and sukun (ْ) on ر show the first syllable 'fir', while the و with sukun makes the long 'aw' sound.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Pharaoh is the one who 'led astray' (أَضَلَّ) his people.
Root meaning 'stand/gather' becomes a noun for 'people' (like a gathered group) by adding vowel marks
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أَضَلَّ (led astray)

steps.m: his people (being led astray by فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show these are Pharaoh's people who he led astray
This is a negative particle that means 'not'. In this ayah, it negates the verb هَدَىٰ (guided), showing that Pharaoh did not guide his people.
Adding وَ at the start connects this phrase to the previous statement, showing contrast between what Pharaoh did (led his people astray) and what he did not do (guide them).

steps.m: to guide or show the right path

steps.n: Added fathas (small diagonal lines) to make it 'he guided' in the past tense
Changed the final ي to ى (alif maqsurah) since this is a past tense verb ending in a weak letter
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show clearly that this is pronounced with a long 'aa' sound at the end.
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowel marks
These are different words - 'banaa' (to build) and 'bani' (sons) are not related through transformation
Added يٰ at the beginning to make it a call or address to someone
Added ى at the end to show possession - 'my sons'

steps.m: Israel (being addressed in يٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ - O Children of Israel)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew, combining two parts: يسرا (struggles/strives) and ايل (God), meaning 'one who struggles with God'. It refers to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) and his descendants.
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the past tense verb that follows it (أَنجَيْنٰكُم - We saved you). It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English to make the statement stronger.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و becomes ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Adding أَ at the start makes it causative - meaning someone makes the action happen to someone else
Adding نَا at the end shows 'we' did the action
Adding كُم shows the action was done to 'you all' (plural)
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or starts. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a vowel sound, making the م sound stronger. The sukun (ْ) shows the word stops at ن.

steps.m: to be hostile, to show enmity

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the final و to create a noun meaning someone who does the action intensively
Changed to show possession/ownership - like saying 'my enemy' or 'enemy of'
Added 2nd person masculine plural possessive suffix
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif shows the action involves two parties making promises to each other
Adding نا shows 'we' did the action

steps.m: we made a mutual promise to you all
steps.n: Adding كُمْ shows who received the promise

steps.m: and we made a mutual promise to you all
steps.n: Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to the previous statement
Basic root letters got vowels to make a past tense verb meaning 'to go to the side'

steps.m: one who goes to the side
steps.n: Changed from verb to noun meaning 'one who does the action'
Added damma to create the noun form
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ط with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: of the mountain (possessed by جَانِبَ - side)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this mountain belongs to or is connected to جَانِبَ (side), making the phrase 'side of the mountain'
أَ at start and sukun in middle makes it mean 'more/most'

steps.m: the right-sided (describing جَانِبَ 'side' in this ayah)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) at start and changed ending to match the word 'side' it describes
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda to middle root letter creates intensive/repetitive meaning
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action. The ْ (sukun) is added before نا to help pronunciation.
Adding conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'
Basic preposition showing something is on top of or over something else
Changed ى to ي to prepare for adding suffix
Added كُمُ to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: to give a special gift or favor

steps.n: Doubling the ن adds emphasis to show the favor is significant

steps.m: the manna (the special food Allah gave as a gift)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to the specific manna that Allah gave to the Children of Israel
The root س-ل-و became سَلْوَىٰ by adding vowels and changing the ending to make it mean a specific bird (quail) that was given as food
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning to make it specific. The س gets doubled (شدّة) when ٱل is added.
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect it to the previous word (المنّ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed from 'he ate' to a command by dropping the first letter and changing the middle vowel to 'u'

steps.m: eat! (command to group) (followed by مِن طَيِّبٰتِ - from the good things)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to make it a command for multiple people. This command is connected to what follows - telling the group to eat from the good things that were provided.

steps.m: from (connecting كُلُوا۟ eat to طَيِّبٰتِ good things)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that connects words to show where something comes from or what it's part of. In this ayah, it connects the command to eat (كُلُوا۟) with what should be eaten (طَيِّبٰتِ - good things).
Added vowels to make a basic adjective
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger
Added feminine plural ending to show multiple good things

steps.m: good things, pure things (object of كُلُوا)
steps.n: Changed ending to show this word is the object of the command 'eat' (كُلُوا)

steps.m: what (connects رَزَقْنٰكُمْ to what comes before)
steps.n: This word connects the verb رَزَقْنٰكُمْ (We provided you) to what comes before it. In this context, it means 'what' or 'that which', showing which things Allah provided.

steps.m: he provided (command object of كُلُوا - eat)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense. This word is related to the command كُلُوا (eat) in the ayah, showing what to eat.

steps.m: we provided (command object of كُلُوا - eat)
steps.n: Added نٰ to show Allah is speaking as 'We'. The sukun (ْ) is added before نٰ to help pronunciation.

steps.m: we provided you all (command object of كُلُوا - eat)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this was provided to multiple people (the Children of Israel being addressed). The sukun at the end shows this is the end of the word.
This is the basic negation particle that tells people not to do something. Here it's connected to the verb تَطْغَوْا۟ (transgress/exceed bounds) to tell people not to do that action.
Added و at the beginning to connect this command with the previous one (كُلُوا۟ - eat). It's like saying 'eat... and do not...'
Connected root letters with fatha marks and changed ي to ى because it's at the end
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and sukun on ط to join letters
Added وْا at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
Added kasra vowel mark to show proper pronunciation
Added pronoun ه with kasra (هِ) at the end to show what the preposition refers to. This connects to غَضَبِى (My anger) in the ayah, showing what will come upon them.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Combined the doubled letters with a shadda
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra

steps.m: and so he descends (consequence of تَطْغَوْا۟)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a result of the previous action (not obeying). This word shows what happens as a result of disobeying in تَطْغَوْا۟.
This is a preposition that comes from the concept of being high/elevated, which evolved to mean 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to a pronoun
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: to become angry, to be filled with anger

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be angry' into the noun 'anger'
Basic word 'anger' becomes 'my anger' by adding ي at the end - this is what falls upon someone in the ayah
This is a question word that became a conditional word meaning 'whoever' when used at the start of a condition. In this ayah, it introduces whoever does the action in the next part.
Added 'and' (و) at the start to connect this with the previous statement. This word points to the person who will receive what comes next.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding ي at the beginning shows this is happening now, not in the past
The ى at the end changes to ي with sukun (ْ) when connecting to pronouns
Added هِ (hi) at the end to show the meaning 'him/it'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the noun form for 'anger'
Added first person possessive suffix ى with kasra before it. This word is the subject of يَحِلَّ (descends/befalls) in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that adds emphasis or certainty to what follows

steps.m: then indeed/so certainly (connects to يَحِلَّ - descends)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَحِلَّ (descends), showing that one action leads to another.

steps.m: he fell down (result of يَحْلِلْ - falls upon)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. This word shows what happens as a result of Allah's anger falling upon someone in this ayah.
The small alif (ٰ) extends the sound of ى to show emphasis on the falling
This is a word that emphasizes what comes after it, like saying 'truly' in English
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: and indeed I (am the one who لَغَفَّارٌ/repeatedly forgives)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show 'I' am doing the action. The ن gets a kasra (ِ) before ى. This word connects to لَغَفَّارٌ (repeatedly forgives) in the ayah to mean 'indeed I am the one who repeatedly forgives'

steps.m: to cover up or remove faults

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: one who constantly covers up faults
steps.n: The doubled letter (ّ) and long ا show this person does this action a lot. The ٌ at the end shows this is describing someone/something.

steps.m: truly one who constantly covers up faults (describing اللَّهِ)
steps.n: The لَ at the beginning adds emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'. This word is describing Allah in this ayah.
This is a word that refers to people in a general way, like saying 'whoever' or 'anyone who'. In this ayah, it refers to the person who repents (تَابَ).
Added ل at the beginning to show this is 'for' someone
Added shadda on ل to make it stronger, showing emphasis like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'

steps.m: to return back from wrong to right
he returned back (from wrong to right)
steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it past tense - 'he returned back'

steps.m: he returned back (from wrong to right)
steps.n: Changed to hollow verb form by adding alif - common for verbs with middle weak letter
Added alif after first letter and fatha marks to show past tense action

steps.m: and he believed (connected to تَابَ - repented)
steps.n: The و connects this action to the previous action of repenting (تَابَ)
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: and he worked (connected to تَابَ and ءَامَنَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this action with the previous actions of repenting and believing
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the pattern to show someone or something that has this quality
Added tanween (ًا) to show this is the object being acted upon. This word is what is being done in the phrase 'did righteous deeds'.
This is a connecting word that shows sequence with a time gap between actions. It connects to the word ٱهْتَدَىٰ (became guided) showing this guidance happened after the previous actions.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and show past tense. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Added اِ at start and ت in middle to show doing the action to yourself (guiding yourself)
Added ى with small alif (ٰ) at end to show proper spelling of weak verb
This is a question word used to ask 'what' about something
Added و at the beginning to connect this question to what came before it
The alif is elongated to add emphasis to the question (this is a pronunciation change that doesn't affect the meaning)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to make someone else rush'
Adding كَ shows who was made to rush. This word answers the question 'what made you rush?' in the ayah
This is a basic preposition showing separation or moving away from something. It connects يٰمُوسَىٰ (O Moses) to his قَوْمِ (people) in this ayah.
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
The root relates to standing or rising up
Changes from root meaning to noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by what comes after it
Added كَ to show these are specifically your people. This word follows عَن (from/about) in the ayah.
This is the name of the prophet Moses (مُوسَى). Since it's a proper name, it doesn't come from an Arabic root.
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation
Added يٰ at the beginning to show someone is calling out to Moses. The long alif (ٰ) at both ends shows this is a call for attention. This word appears when Allah is directly addressing Moses.
Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding the vowel marks (fathas)
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: they/them (referring to the people who went astray in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a basic pronoun that refers to a group of people. In this ayah, it's used by Prophet Musa to refer to his people who he left behind.
This is a demonstrative word used to point to things that are far away. It's a frozen form without a root.
Adding ء shows this word is for plural (more than one)

steps.m: those (pointing to people or things) (subject of عَلَىٰٓ)
steps.n: Adding ِ shows this word is connected to what comes after it in the sentence. Here it connects to عَلَىٰٓ (upon).
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something is on top of, upon, or over something else
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (ىٰ) to show it's elongated when reading
The small alif (ٓ) shows a slight pause in reading. Here it connects to the previous word أُو۟لَآءِ (those), forming the phrase 'those upon'

steps.m: to track or follow a trace

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to create a noun meaning the actual mark or trail left behind
Added kasra at the end because it follows the preposition على
Added ى at the end to show the trace belongs to me

steps.m: to rush/hurry eagerly (often implying haste or impatience)

steps.n: Added vowels to make it into a past tense verb showing an emotional state of eagerness or impatience
Changed from third person 'he hurried' to first person 'I hurried' by adding تُ suffix. This is Musa speaking to Allah (رَبِّ) in this ayah.

steps.m: and I rushed/hurried eagerly (speaking to رَبِّ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the start to connect this action with what came before in the ayah. Shows this rushing was in addition to following his people's tracks mentioned earlier.
This is a preposition that shows direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns at the end

steps.m: to you (male) (subject of عَجِلْتُ - I hastened)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is directed to one male person. This word connects to عَجِلْتُ (I hastened) in the ayah, showing who was being hastened to.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to its full potential
one who nurtures and cares completely (Lord)
steps.n: Doubling of middle letter ب intensifies meaning
Adding kasra at end makes this a call/address to Lord
This is the past tense form of the verb. It tells us that something happened in the past.  The 'َ' vowels show the past tense and the specific grammatical person (he).
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed ending to ى because it's a special type of word (called a weak word)

steps.m: so that you become pleased (purpose of عَجِلْتُ/I rushed)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this is the reason or purpose for the action 'I rushed' (عَجِلْتُ) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added vowels to make past tense. This word introduces what Allah said to Musa.
The و changes to ا, which is a common change with this type of word
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain

steps.m: so indeed (connects to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The فَ connects this statement to what came before (قَالَ - he said), showing it's a response

steps.m: so indeed we (speaking to قَوْمَكَ - your people)
steps.n: The ا (alif) is added to make it 'we/us' instead of just 'indeed', connecting to how we are speaking
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the past tense verb فَتَنَّا (we tested) that follows it. It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' before a statement in English.

steps.m: to test or try (especially with hardship)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to show the testing was more intense or severe
Adding ا at the end changes it from 'he' to 'we'
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'. This word is the object of فَتَنَّا (we tested) in this ayah.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb فَتَنَّا (we tested)
Added كَ at the end to show these are 'your' people. The word remains the object being tested in the sentence.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from, like saying 'from' in English. In this ayah, it connects with بَعْدِكَ to mean 'after you'.
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) that helps connect this word smoothly to the next word بَعْدِكَ when reading
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukoon) to the root letters to make it mean 'after'
Added كَ at the end to show it means 'after you'. The ِ before كَ shows this word follows مِنۢ (from) in the sentence.
Added fatha on first letter and shadda on second letter to make the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning to show causing someone else to lose their way

steps.m: and he led astray (connected to فَتَنَّا - we tested)
steps.n: Added وَ meaning 'and' to connect this action with the previous statement about testing

steps.m: and he led them astray (referring to قَوْمَكَ - your people)
steps.n: Added هُمُ at the end to show who was led astray - referring back to 'your people' mentioned earlier
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who stays awake at night
steps.n: Changed to active participle pattern showing who does the action
Added suffix showing connection to a place (Samaria)
Added the definite article 'the'. This person is the subject (doer) of the verb أَضَلَّ (led astray) in this ayah.
Added ُّ to show this is the subject doing the action

steps.m: he returned (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word shows who did the action in قَالَ (he said) later in the verse.

steps.m: so he returned (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action happened as a result of or after something else. In this verse, it shows Moses returned to his people after what happened before.
This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out [of water]', referring to how Moses was found in the river as a baby. In this ayah, Moses is the one who returned to his people.
Removed the small vertical mark (ٓ) above the alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end of Moses' name
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above the alif maksura to indicate a slight elongation in pronunciation
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows direction or destination
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it. Here it connects to 'his people' (قَوْمِهِۦ) to show where Moses returned to.
Changes verb 'to stand' into noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is possessed by someone and comes after the word 'to' (إِلَىٰ)
Added هِۦ at the end to show these are Moses's people
Root meaning 'anger' becomes the noun 'anger' with vowel marks fatHa and sukoon
Added ان to show intense temporary state. This describes Moses's emotional state when he returned to his people.
Changed the alif to an alif maksura (ٰ) which is just a writing variation with no meaning change
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show the emotional state
This word describes Moses's emotional state when he returned to his people. It works with the word غَضْبٰنَ (angry) to show Moses was both angry and deeply saddened.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes after fatha - this is a common Arabic spelling rule
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun for a group of people who stand/gather together
Added kasra at the end to show this word is affected by a preposition or possession

steps.m: O my people! (Moses speaking to his people)
steps.n: The يٰ at the start is used to call out to someone. In this ayah, Moses is calling out to his people after returning to find them worshipping the calf.
Question particle that turns a statement into a question

steps.m: did not (questioning يَعِدْكُمْ - he promised you)
steps.n: Added لَمْ which negates the present tense verb that follows (يَعِدْكُمْ). Together with أَ, it forms a questioning negative: 'Did he not promise you?'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start. The initial و is dropped because it's a weak letter. The middle vowel changes to kasra and final letter gets sukun.

steps.m: he promises you all (in response to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show the promise is being made to multiple people. This word comes after قَالَ (he said) in the ayah.
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter with shadda to show intensity of the nurturing quality
The ُ (damma) shows this word is the subject/doer in the sentence. The shadda doubles the ب to show intensity/mastery.

steps.m: your Lord (addressing the Children of Israel)
steps.n: The كُمْ shows this is addressing multiple people (Moses' people). This word appears in Moses' speech to his people after returning from Mount Sinai.
Changed from the root letters to the noun 'promise' by adding the vowel marks fatHa and sukoon

steps.m: a promise (object of يَعِدْ - he promised)
steps.n: Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this is the object being promised. This word is what was promised in يَعِدْ (he promised) from the previous verse.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it into a descriptive word
Added tanween alif to show this word is describing the promise (وَعْدًا) that Allah made to them
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a past tense verb. The و becomes ا because it's a hollow verb.
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of something before it

steps.m: has it become long? (questioning the long wait)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question. In this ayah, Moses is asking his people if the time of his absence was too long.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to pronouns
Added كُمُ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
The verb عَهَدَ (to make a promise) changed to the noun عَهْدُ (promise/covenant). It's like changing 'promised' to 'a promise' in English. Here it's asking if the promise has become too long.
Added ال to specify this particular covenant that was mentioned before
This is a special kind of 'or' used in questions to present alternatives. Here it connects back to the previous question 'Did not your Lord promise you?' to ask 'Or did you want...?'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he made want/he caused to want
steps.n: Adding أَ at the start makes the verb affect someone else
Adding تُ at the end makes it mean 'I' did the action
Changed from 'I' (تُ) to 'you all' (تُّمْ) at the end to show multiple people are being spoken to
This is a connecting word that introduces what will happen next in the sentence. Here it connects to the verb يَحِلَّ (descend/befall) to show what might happen.
Added vowels (َ) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he descended'
Changed from past tense 'he descended' to a command 'descend!' by changing vowels and doubling middle letter
Added يَ at beginning to show present/future tense. This word is connected to عَلَيْكُمْ (upon you) in the ayah, showing what is descending.
This is the basic preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting to the next part. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this affects multiple people being spoken to. This word is receiving the action of يَحِلَّ (descends) in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and turn the action into a noun
Added tanween to show this is the subject doing the action. In this ayah, the anger is described as something that would descend upon them from their Lord.
This is a preposition showing origin or source. It connects رَبِّكُمْ (your Lord) to what comes before it.
The م gets doubled (gets a shadda) because it comes after a word ending in ن, making it blend together when spoken

steps.m: to nurture, care for, or guide to completion

steps.n: Doubling the ب makes it stronger - shows complete mastery/lordship
Added kasra at the end to show possession
Added كَ at the end to mean 'your' (when speaking to one male)
Added كُمْ instead of كَ to show 'your' means many people + added shadda (ّ) at start to emphasize 'indeed'

steps.m: to break a promise/to go against

steps.n: Changed to make it mean 'to actively break/violate something' by adding أ at the start
Adds 'so/then' to connect this action to what happened before
Makes it clear that 'I' did the action

steps.m: to promise or set a time/place to meet

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to create a word for where or when something happens
Doubles the م to add emphasis to the meaning
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to something
Added ى at the end to show this is my appointment. This word is what was broken/not kept in the phrase 'you broke/failed to keep'.
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
The middle letter و changed to ا and وا was added at the end to show many people did the action
Basic negative particle that negates the verb after it. Here it negates أَخْلَفْنَا (we broke), making it mean 'we did not break'.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adds 'أ' at start to make it mean 'deliberately/actively breaking' something, like a promise

steps.m: we broke (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Adding نا at the end changes 'he broke' to 'we broke' - showing we did the action. This word comes after 'they said' (قَالُوا) to show what they said.

steps.m: to promise or set a time/place to meet

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to show where or when something happens, like changing 'promise' to 'place/time of promise'

steps.m: your appointment (object of أَخْلَفْنَا/we failed to keep)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show it belongs to 'you'. This word is what was broken/failed in the phrase 'we failed to keep your appointment'
Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'power/control' instead of the verb 'to control'. Like changing 'run' to 'running'.
Added نا at the end to show it belongs to 'us'. The ِ (kasra) before نا connects it smoothly to the word.

steps.m: by/with our power (explaining how they did something)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to show this explains how something was done. In this ayah, it explains how they didn't break their promise by their own power/choice.
This is the basic word that shows contrast between ideas
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain
Adding ا ('alif) at the end changes 'but' to 'but we', making it about ourselves
Adding وَ connects this to the previous statement
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he carried' to 'was carried' by adding dots above (damma) and below (kasra) with a double letter mark (shadda)
Added نَا with long alif to show 'we' as the subject, with sukun before it

steps.m: to carry or bear a heavy load

steps.n: Changed the action into a noun representing the thing being carried
Changed from singular to plural form by adding أَ at the start and changing the middle vowel pattern

steps.m: burdens (object of حُمِّلْنَا - we were made to bear)
steps.n: Added the accusative case ending because this word is the object of the verb حُمِّلْنَا (we were made to bear)
This is a fundamental Arabic preposition that shows source, origin, or starting point. It has no root or derivational steps as it's a particle (حرف)

The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a word ending in a vowel (مَا). In Arabic, when مِن follows a word ending in a vowel, it gets a shadda for easier pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changed from an action to a thing by adding ة at the end and ِ on the first letter
The ِ at the end shows this decoration belongs to the people (القوم) mentioned next in the verse

steps.m: to stand together, to rise up as one

steps.n: The root meaning of standing together becomes a noun for a group that stands as one unit - a people or folk
The word 'people' gets 'the' added at the start (ال) and a kasra ending (ِ) because it shows ownership of the ornaments
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Added ها to show what was thrown (it/her)
Added فَ to show this action happened as a result of something before it
Starting with the basic demonstrative word meaning 'this/that'
Adding ل with kasra changes it to mean 'like' or 'similar to'
Adding ك makes it specifically mean 'that' (pointing to something far) rather than 'this'

steps.m: and so/thus like that (connected to فَقَذَفْنٰهَا)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this is a result or consequence of the previous action (throwing) mentioned in فَقَذَفْنٰهَا (so we threw it)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start and putting a sukun on the ل shows this is about making something happen to an object

steps.m: to stay awake at night, to converse at night

steps.n: Root letters connected with fathas (a vowels) to make a noun meaning 'staying up at night'

steps.m: one who stays up at night
steps.n: Changed pattern to show someone who does this action
Added the relative suffix يّ to show connection to a place (Samaria)
Added the definite article ال and doubled the س with shadda. This word is the subject of the verb أَلْقَى (he threw/cast).
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the verb
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the verb causative - it changes from doing the action yourself to making something/someone else do it. The word عِجْلًا (calf) is what was brought out.

steps.m: then he brought out (عِجْلًا - a calf)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this action happened as a result of or following previous events. Here it connects to the story sequence.
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هُ (with damma) which means 'him'

steps.m: to/for them (referring to the people who made the calf)
steps.n: Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them' (masculine plural)
Added kasra and sukun to make it a noun meaning a specific animal. This pattern is used for some animal names.

steps.m: a calf (object of أَخْرَجَ - he brought out)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha (ًا) at the end to show this is the object being brought out in the phrase 'he brought out for them a calf'
Connected the root letters together with fatha vowels to create a basic noun for a physical thing

steps.m: a body (object of أَخْرَجَ - he brought out)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being brought out in the phrase 'he brought out for them a body'
Combined the basic particle ل (for/to) with the pronoun ه (it) to show something belongs to or is for something
Added shadda on ل to emphasize the meaning, showing this is definitely or surely for it
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a writing style to show the pronoun هُ more clearly - it doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to make a sound (especially of cattle)
a mooing sound (of the golden calf عِجْلًا)
steps.n: Root letters got vowels and ending sound (-un) to make a noun meaning 'mooing' from the basic idea of cattle sounds
Added vowel marks and changed و to ا. This makes it past tense.
Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural)

steps.m: and so they said (following the previous action in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this happened as a result of what came before - after he brought out the calf for them
This is the basic demonstrative element used to point to something. Like pointing with your finger and saying 'that' in English.
Adding ه at the start makes it specifically point to something nearby, like saying 'this' instead of 'that' in English.
Adding the small alif (ٰ) above the ه and the fatha (َ) on ذ helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
The final alif is written with a special form (آ) but pronounced the same way.
Changed first letter to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added ُ to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: your god (referring to the golden calf they made)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this god belongs to the people being spoken to (the Israelites who made the calf)
Changed first letter to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to make the 'aa' sound longer
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
This is the base form of the proper name Moses, borrowed from Hebrew/Ancient Egyptian. It doesn't follow typical Arabic root patterns since it's a foreign name.
Added the basic vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name correctly
Added the ى at the end which is common for foreign names in Arabic
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show that the sound is stretched slightly when pronounced

steps.m: to forget or leave something behind

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make 'he forgot' - connects to what happened after they said (فَقَالُوا)
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of what came before. In the ayah, this follows after they said something.
This particle turns a statement into a question, like adding a question mark in English
Added فَ which shows this question follows from what happened before

steps.m: Then do they not...? (introduces a rhetorical question)
steps.n: Added لَا which makes this negative. Together these particles create a rhetorical question expecting the answer 'yes they should!'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Present tense form starts with يَ. The middle letter أ is dropped and the ending changes to ى, making it easier to say
Added ون at the end to show multiple people doing the action. The و replaces the ى due to Arabic spelling rules.
Starting with the two separate words that will combine

steps.m: that not (shows something won't happen in يَرْجِعُ)
steps.n: The ن from أَن combines with the ل from لَا to make a doubled ل (shown by the ّ mark). This word connects to يَرْجِعُ (returns) to show that something won't return.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on ر, kasra under ج, and damma at end. This word is part of what they see (يَرَوْنَ) in the ayah.
Adds a 'u' sound at the end to show it's the main verb
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to pronouns at the end

steps.m: to them (returns to them in يَرْجِعُ)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to the verb يَرْجِعُ (returns) in the ayah.

steps.m: a saying, statement (what is being returned in يَرْجِعُ)
steps.n: The root letters for 'speak' became a noun meaning 'a saying' by adding the vowels. This is what's being returned in يَرْجِعُ (returns).
Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb يَرْجِعُ (returns)
Starting with the basic negation particle لا which means 'not'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous phrase. This connects to the verb يَمْلِكُ (possesses/controls) to say 'and does not possess'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adds ُ at the end to show this is the main doer in the sentence
This is the basic preposition that shows direction or purpose
Added the pronoun هُمْ which means 'them' to show who the action is for
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a basic noun
The middle letter gets doubled to show intensity
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon. This word is what is being possessed/controlled in the phrase 'does not possess/control harm'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَمْلِكُ (possesses/controls) into 'does not possess/control'

steps.m: and not (connects to previous negation أَلَّا)
steps.n: The و connects this negative to the previous negative أَلَّا (that not), showing these are two things that don't happen
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to turn the concept of benefiting into a noun meaning 'benefit'
Added tanween (ًا) to show this is the thing being controlled/possessed in the phrase 'does not possess/control any benefit'
This is the basic particle that shows certainty or completion of an action
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, and added sukun at the end to show it's connected to what comes next
Added fathas to make past tense form. This word is the main verb that introduces what Harun said to his people.
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to the people Aaron was speaking to in this part of the story.
This is the basic form of the proper name Aaron (هٰرُون). The long alif (ٰ) shows it's a non-Arabic name adapted into Arabic.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show that Aaron is the one doing the action (saying) in this verse. This connects to the verb قَالَ (he said) that appears before this word.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it combines with قَبْلُ (before) to mean 'from before'

steps.m: to come before or in front of something

steps.n: The root letters were given vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to make the word mean 'before' instead of the action of coming before something
